summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Decision Making and Living Arrangements – Where the father and mother both sought sole decision making authority and for the children to live with them – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer supported the father’s position – Where both the father and the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought that the children spend no time with the mother – Where the Court found that the mother was an unacceptable risk of harm to the children – Where the Court is not satisfied that supervised contact would ameliorate the unacceptable risk – Where the Court orders that the children spend no time with the mother – Where the Court orders that the father retain sole decision making and the children live with him. FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Restraints – Where the father sought the mother be restrained from contacting, approaching within 100 metres of, and posting on social media about him and the children – Where the mother sought similar restraints against the father –Where the Court found that the children’s safety would be promoted by the restraints as sought by the father – Where the Court also made a restraint that the mother cannot direct, cause, encourage or facilitate any third party from attempting any behaviour that she herself is restrained from. FAMILY LAW –PARENTING – Harmful Proceedings Order – Where the father sought an order under s 102QAC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) – Where the mother failed to advance a formal position in relation to the exercise of the Court’s authority under the Act – Where the mother’s conduct and constant filing of applications in this Court, caused the Court to find that there are reasonable grounds to believe that the father and the children would suffer ‘harm’ if the mother was allowed to instituted further proceedings without leave of the Court – Where the Court makes an order under s 102QAC of the Act – Where the father was silent as to his wishes relating to notification of any future attempted applications by the mother – Where the Court made orders that the father ought to only be notified where an application by the respondent under s 102QAE of the Act has been dismissed by the Court.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

INTRODUCTION

This complex case is about four children, namely W born 2010, X born 2011, Y born 2013, and Z born 2017 (collectively “the children”) who currently live with the Applicant Father (“father”) and neither spend time with, nor communicate with the Respondent Mother (“mother”) (collectively “the parents”). The Court was tasked with deciding where the children live, how parental responsibility was to be allocated, and what time and communication would take place between the children and the parent with whom they did not live.
The main striking feature of this case was the vulnerability of both parents, and each of the children. The next most striking feature was the disconcerting inability of the mother, who chose to represent herself, to regulate her own emotions in the public setting of the courtroom.
After considering all the evidence the Court has, with deep regret, found that it is not in the best interests of the children to either spend time with, or communicate with the mother. Even supervised time, which has been tried in the past without success, is not in the best interests of the children.
Another distinct issue was whether the mother should be permitted to pursue an application to commence property proceedings out of time where she did not comply with the orders of the Court to formally seek the same but included evidence about the same in her affidavits. It is noted that the father led no evidence about any potential property proceedings.
The necessity to bring these long-running proceedings to an end, for the sake of the children, seemed to be obvious to everyone except the mother, and the challenge in achieving this is a formidable one. The harmful proceedings order is an attempt to accomplish this.
These reasons for judgement are intentionally as concise as the evidence permits. The focus will be on the main issues justifying the orders made. To have set out all of the evidence, despite much of that having no direct bearing on the orders made, is unnecessary, insensitive and undignified for this family.

BACKGROUND

The father is forty-five years old, currently resides in Suburb K and works part-time as a professional. The father is not presently in a relationship. The mother is forty-two, resides in Suburb L, and is currently unemployed and in receipt of a Disability Support Pension. The mother was in a relationship with Mr B but the date of commencement of this, and its current status, is unclear from the evidence. In cross-examination the mother told the Court that they were no longer speaking. This does not mean that they are no longer in a relationship. The parents met in 2007, married in 2008, formally separated in October 2019 and were divorced in 2023.
The children were represented at different times by two experienced Independent Children’s Lawyers. The first one was granted leave to withdraw after what the Court finds was a completely unjustified campaign of vilification by the mother. The second one discharged the role through to the completion of the proceedings, acting professionally, courteously, and with the utmost respect and sensitivity towards the mother.
Despite having the benefit of multiple orders under s 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), the Court was left in no doubt, based on the mother’s own admissions, that she chose to represent herself.
The Court was very conscious of the mother’s acknowledged diagnosis of neurodivergence. The Court enquired of the mother about what could be done to make the Court hearing a more bearable experience for her. Prior to the mother stepping into the witness box, the following exchange occurred:
HIS HONOUR: ... there’s lots I hope that I can do to make the cross-examination experience easier for you. Now, for example, can I just go through the things that I would like to do to support you in cross-examination today? So the first thing is, if you have a support person, that support person can sit with you in the witness box. I’m going to make sure that counsel and Mr MacDiarmid, who will ask you questions, will ask short single questions. I’m going to make sure that we have ‑ ‑ ‑

[MS MAVRIDIS]: I’m not prepared.

HIS HONOUR: Well, may I finish, [Ms Mavridis]? I just want to tell you what I think we can do to support you. Right. I’m going to suggest that we take at least a 10 minute break for every hour of cross-examination. I’m going to make sure that [Mr Mavridis] is not in your line of sight, that is to say, that you cannot see him and that he cannot see you. I’m going to give you permission to stand up and move around at times if that’s what you would like. I will take no adverse inference if you are not looking at the person who is asking you questions. If you say to me that you are uncomfortable, I will try to accommodate you.

If, for example, it’s too hot in here, too cold in here, the lights are too bright or whatever, I will do the best I can to accommodate you. I’m going to consciously slow things down so that you have time to listen and think and respond. So – and quite frankly, anything else that you ask me to do that might help make this experience something that you can manage, seriously, I will take it into account. But, [Ms Mavridis], one thing I cannot do is to stop this court case.

(Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 102 line 35 to p.103 line 17).
The mother declined the accommodations offered by the Court, other than regular rest breaks. The mother’s dissatisfaction with, and deep distrust for, the Court process in all its constituent people and parts, was frequently expressed by her.
It is important to acknowledge that the hearing must have been exceedingly difficult for the mother, being neurodivergent and representing herself. It is equally important to acknowledge how difficult it must have been for every other person in the courtroom to cope with the mother’s frequent emotional dysregulation. For the father, in particular, it may well have been re-traumatising.
The Court also acknowledges the challenges that both parents would have experienced in parenting neurodiverse young children, as well as the heroic efforts of the father in parenting these children largely alone after separation. In this case, of course, the mother is also neurodiverse. Of itself, neurodiversity is not inconsistent with good parenting. Each neurodiverse child, and parent, is different. Each case must be decided on its own facts.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

The litigation saga between the parents commenced on 5 October 2022 when the father filed an Application for Final Orders. Thirty-nine orders and three interim judgments have been made during the course of the proceedings. One-hundred-and-sixty-eight documents have been filed. By virtue of the length and complexity of these proceedings, these reasons for judgment will only traverse materially relevant orders and determinations.
On 3 November 2022, the proceedings came before Senior Judicial Registrar Hayward for an interim hearing. Orders by consent were made which provided for the children to live with the father and spend three-hours per fortnight with the mother under professional supervision. A specific restraint was made prohibiting the mother from posting any comments or material whatsoever in relation to the proceedings on social media. Additionally, an Independent Children’s Lawyer was appointed, and a Child Impact Report was ordered.
On 18 April 2023, Judicial Registrar Bardetta ordered a Family Report pursuant to s 62G of the Act and listed the matter for a Compliance and Readiness Hearing. It is noted that the parents experienced delays beyond their control with the commencement of the professionally supervised time between the mother and the children, with such time expected to commence in May 2023 (Orders 18 April 2023, Notation D).
On 31 October 2023, Judicial Registrar Bardetta made orders by consent to suspend Z’s supervised time with the mother following concerns raised by the father and Independent Children’s Lawyer relating to potential behavioural difficulties.
On 29 November 2023, Deputy Registrar Magee transferred the proceedings to Division 1 of this Court.
On 13 November 2023, Senior Judicial Registrar Hayward suspended the mother’s supervised time with the children pending further order. The Court was informed that the mother terminated her legal representation due to the cost of same and she has applied for a grant of legal aid.
On 11 April 2023, Senior Judicial Registrar Hayward ordered the Independent Children’s Lawyer to apply to Legal Aid New South Wales for a Specific Issues Report for a psychiatrist to assess the mother’s capacity to understand the nature and conduct of these proceedings and whether the appointment of a litigation guardian is necessary. The Court observed through its notations that the mother has used the assistance of a “McKenzie friend” in the past, such that on 13 December 2023 her sister assisted her and on 11 April 2024 Mr B assisted her (Orders 11 April 2023, Notation C and E). The Court notes that Mr B is “also known [by another name]” (Mavridis & Mavridis (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 440 at [3] (“Mavridis (No 2)”). Notation E sets out that “the Court did not find [Mr B’s] comments helpful or conducive to the conduct of the proceedings”.
On 11 October 2024, Senior Judicial Hayward made orders suspending her previous orders relating to the ordering of a Specific Issues Report in relation to the mother’s capacity to understand the nature and conduct of the proceedings.
On 12 February 2025, the mother filed an Amended Response to Initiating Application which sought an order pursuant to s 79 of the Act.
On 19 February 2025, Campton J set the matter down for Final Hearing before me to commence on 7 April 2025. His Honour noted that “[t]o progress relief adjusting property pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) the mother requires an indulgence by way of leave pursuant to s 44(3) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)” (Orders made 19 February 2025, Notation G).
On 7 April 2025, the Court was advised that the mother terminated her legal representation on 4 April 2025. I granted leave for her solicitor and barrister to withdraw from the proceedings and vacated the final hearing to provide procedural fairness to the mother. I made an order pursuant to s 102NA of the Act and ordered an updating Family Report. The mother foreshadowed bringing an interim application seeking to commence property proceedings out of time, for her time with the children to resume supervised by the maternal grandparents and to discharge the Independent Children’s Lawyer. I listed the matter for an interim hearing on 20 June 2025 in relation to the mother’s foreshadowed application and a final hearing to commence on 7 August 2026. The mother was advised that should she not be ready to proceed for the final hearing commencing on 7 August 2026, that the matter may proceed to finality on an undefended basis.
On 23 April 2026, I listed the matter for mention upon receipt of correspondence from Court Children’s Services which noted that the father declined to facilitate the children’s attendance upon the Court Child Expert for interviews for the updating Family Report (Orders 23 April 2025, Notation A).
On 28 April 2026, I ordered that the Court Child Expert preparing the updating Family Report decide, using her absolute discretion, whether or not to conduct observations between the children and the mother.
On 5 May 2025, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding which sought, amongst other things, to discharge the Independent Children’s Lawyer and to have a new Independent Children’s Lawyer appointed, for the Court to refer the father for fraud, for the Court to make a finding that the mother has not been clinically diagnosed with a mental disorder, for her time with the children to be immediately reinstated and for the matter to be referred for independent review or case management oversight.
On 6 May 2025, Deputy Registrar Bucknall listed the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025 for interim hearing before me.
On 7 May 2025, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding which sought that the property located at M Street, Suburb K (“the former matrimonial home”) be sold and the proceeds be held in trust. Additionally, the mother sought that the net proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home be split 70/30 in her favour.
On 12 May 2025, Senior Judicial Registrar Bardetta listed the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 7 May 2025 for interim hearing before me.
On 20 June 2025, an interim hearing was conducted. During the listing, the Independent Children’s Lawyer sought to withdraw from the proceedings and in the circumstances of this case, I granted such leave and ordered that a new Independent Children’s Lawyer be appointed. Orders 4–9 and 11 of the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025 were stood over to be considered during the final hearing. Additionally, the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 7 May 2025 in relation to property proceedings was stood over to the final hearing contingent upon the mother amending the application to seek leave to commence property proceedings out of time. Pursuant to s 68B of the Act and pending further order, I restrained the mother from causing her then partner, Mr B from having any involvement whatsoever with the proceedings. Interim judgment was otherwise reserved.
On 24 June 2025, the mother made an oral application for leave to adduce further evidence in relation to the interim hearing conducted on 20 June 2025. The Court considered the mother’s application and found that the evidence the mother sought to lead was not fresh evidence, the interests of natural justice did not require this evidence, the evidence was unlikely to affect the result of the hearing and the father would have been prejudiced by allowing the evidence to be taken into consideration. The Court’s reasons for the dismissal of the mother’s oral application are found in Mavridis (No 2). The Court then proceeded to hand down its decision in response to the interim hearing. I dismissed the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025, save for Orders 4–9 and 11 therein, and restrained the mother from allowing Mr B to have any involvement whatsoever with the proceedings (see Mavridis & Mavridis [2025] FedCFamC1F 425 at [43] (“Mavridis”)).
On 16 July 2025, I released the Updating Family Report of Court Child Expert Ms C (“the Expert”) dated 7 July 2025 (“the Updated Family Report”). The mother did not appear on this occasion.
On 4 August 2025, the mother filed two separate Applications in a Proceeding. The first, sought that I recuse myself, for the father’s solicitor to be removed from the proceedings, for the Independent Children’s Lawyer to be reviewed for misconduct and misuse of subpoena material., for the mother’s time with the children to be reinstated, Mr B to be reinstated as her support person, and that she be granted leave to file her application to commence property proceedings and for such proceedings to be heard before a new judicial officer (“the mother’s recusal application”). The second sought a stay of some of my orders from 20 June 2025, which related to the reserving of my judgment over a previous application filed by the mother and the restraint relating to Mr B (“the mother’s stay application”).
On 5 August 2025, Deputy Registrar Taylor listed both of the mother’s Applications in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025 before me for determination at the final hearing commencing on 7 August 2025.
On 7 August 2025, I heard the mother’s recusal and stay application. I dismissed in part, the mother’s recusal application, save for an order sought about the removal of the father’s legal representative on the basis of breaches of undertaking, the dissemination of material, and collusion with the Independent Children’s Lawyer (the mother’s recusal application, Order 3). I dismissed the entirety of the mother’s stay application. Costs relation to the mother’s recusal and stay applications were reserved.
On 8 August 2025, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding which sought, again, that I recuse myself (“the mother’s second recusal application”). The mother also sought that the final hearing be stayed pending the determination of her two Applications in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025, that the Court declare its “refusal to hear the recusal and stay application on 7 August 2025 invalid for failure to determine a properly listed objection before continuing with the substantive proceedings” (Application in a Proceeding filed 8 August 2025, Order 3), and that all orders made during the final hearing on 7 August 2025 be declared void. The Court notes that the only orders made on 7 August 2025 were orders dealing with the mother’s recusal and stay applications.
On 11 August 2025, counsel for the father did not appear at the trial, despite being midway through cross-examination of the mother, and instead attempted to send alternate counsel to complete the cross-examination. The Court did not provide the father’s counsel leave to withdraw from the proceedings, and thus the matter was caused to be adjourned. I provided the mother leave to discuss the proceedings with any legal representative she may engage prior to the resumption of the hearing, despite her being part way through cross-examination. The matter was stood over for a further three-days commencing 17 November 2025.
On 12 August 2025, I delivered reasons for judgment in relation to the mother’s recusal application, stay application, and second recusal application (Mavridis & Mavridis (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 540 (“Mavridis (No 3)”).
On 19 November 2025, the mother expressed on multiple occasions that she is not prepared to make final oral submissions to the Court. I permitted the mother to file and serve final written submissions and a bundle of documents she sought to tender which provide evidence that there had been fraudulent documents produced in the proceedings by 5:00 pm on 21 November 2026, and thereafter my judgment was reserved.

COMPETING PROPOSALS

The father

The father’s proposed orders are contained in his Outline of Case Document filed 2 August 2025 (“father’s orders sought”). The father sought that he retain sole decision-making responsibility for the children, for the children to live with him, and for the children to spend no time with the mother. Further, the father sought that the mother be restrained from contacting the father or the children’s treaters, going within 100 metres of where the father and children reside, work or attend, and from posting any material in relation to the children or father on social media. Lastly and separately, the father sought an order pursuant to s102QAC of the Act.

The mother

The mother’s proposed orders are contained in her Amended Application to Final Orders filed 7 August 2025 (“mother’s orders sought”). The mother sought sole parental responsibility for the children, for the children to live with her and for a moratorium of time with the father for a period of six-months. Further, the mother sought orders that the children engage with a child psychologist for ongoing counselling during the moratorium period with the father. Additionally, the mother sought restraints on the father to prohibit him from contacting her or the children, attending within 100 metres of the mother or children, and posting content on social media related to the mother, children or proceedings.
The mother also sought that the parties engage in property proceedings under the Act. She sought that she be granted sole occupancy of the former matrimonial home until such time as Z turns 18 years old, or until he completes secondary education. Further, the mother sought costs on an indemnity basis. The Court notes that the mother did not seek an order for leave pursuant to s 44(3) of the Act.
The mother’s final position was reaffirmed in her written submissions dated 21 November 2025 (“the mother’s written submissions”) at headings 7 and 8, which were largely consistent with her position expressed above. However, within the mother’s written submissions, she particularised that she sought an indulgence of the Court pursuant to s 44(3) of the Act.

Interim applications on foot

Orders 4–9 and 11 of the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025 were stood over for consideration at the final hearing pursuant to the orders made 24 June 2025. These orders relate to the mother pursuing a finding that she has not been clinically diagnosed with a mental disorder, the rejection of any evidence which refers to such a diagnosis, and having various agencies informed of the incorrect diagnosis. Additionally, the mother sought that the matter be referred for independent review to prevent further misuse of mental health allegations or agency processes.
Order 3 of the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025 was also stood over to the final hearing which relates to the removal of the father’s solicitor “due to serious breaches of legal undertaking, unauthorised dissemination of subpoenaed material, and collusive conduct with the former ICL” (see Mavridis (No 3) at [22]).

The Independent Children’s Lawyer

The Independent Children’s Lawyer filed an Outline of Case Document on 6 August 2025 (“the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s position”) which sets out at paragraph 28 that if the allegations summarised by the father are upheld, then the orders sought by him are “completely appropriate”. This was confirmed during the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s closing submissions where he said, “having seen the evidence play out in this court, having heard from [the mother] and from [the father’s counsel], I have to say my view is that I support the orders that are promoted by the father.” (Transcript 19 November 2025, p.276 lines 22–24).

EVIDENCE BEFORE THE COURT

In support of his case, the father relied on the following documents:
(1) Outline of Case Document filed 2 August 2025;

(2) Amended Initiating Application filed 26 May 2025;

(3) Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 16 June 2025;

(4) His Affidavit filed 16 June 2025;

(5) His Affidavit filed 25 July 2025;

(6) Affidavit of Ms N filed 27 March 2025;

(7) Affidavit of Ms P filed 13 November 2023;

(8) Notice of Child Abuse, Family Violence or Risk filed 30 September 2022; and

(9) Various exhibits tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits A1–A45.

In support of her case, the mother relied on the following documents:
(1) Amended Response to Final Orders filed 7 August 2025;

(2) Orders 4–9 and 11 of the Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025;

(3) Order 3 of the Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025;

(4) Her Affidavit filed 10 April 2024;

(5) Her Affidavit filed 5 May 2025;

(6) Affidavit of Ms E filed on 5 May 2025;

(7) Affidavit of Mr F filed on 5 May 2025;

(8) Affidavit of Ms G filed on 5 May 2025;

(9) Her Affidavit filed 20 June 2025;

(10) Her Affidavit filed 4 August 2025;

(11) Her Affidavit filed 7 August 2025;

(12) Her Affidavit filed 21 November 2025;

(13) Affidavit and annexures to the Affidavit of Dr O filed 10 March 2025;

(14) Affidavit of Mr Q filed on 14 March 2025;

(15) Written submissions dated 21 November 2025; and

(16) Various exhibits tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits R1–R13.

In support of his case, the Independent Children’s Lawyer relied on the following material:
(1) Outline of Case Document filed 6 August 2025;

(2) Child Impact Report of Court Child Expert, Ms R dated 20 March 2023;

(3) Family Report of Court Child Expert, Ms C dated 19 September 2023;

(4) Updated Family Report of Court Child Expert, Ms C dated 7 July 2025; and

(5) Various exhibits tendered throughout the hearing which became Exhibits ICL1–ICL3.

During the trial, the Court heard from several witnesses. In the father’s case, I heard from him, Ms N, and Ms P. In the mother’s case I heard from her only. All parties also had the opportunity to cross-examine the Expert.

APPLICABLE LAW

The applicable law is found in Part VII of the Act. The Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) (“the Amendment Act”) came into effect on 6 May 2024 and made substantive amendments to the previous Act which apply to these proceedings. In determining parenting matters under Part VII of the Act the Court must still regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration: s 60CA.
The objects and principles of Part VII are set out at s 60B:

60B Objects of Part

The objects of this Part are:

(a) to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety; and

(b) to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child done at New York on 20 November 1989.

In regard to parental responsibility, the presumption of equal shared parental responsibility as created by the previous s 61DA of the Act has been abolished. Instead, s 61D(3) of the Act provides the Court with the ability to deal with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions in relation to children as follows:

61D Parenting orders and parental responsibility

...

(3) A parenting order that deals with the allocation of responsibility for making decisions about major long-term issues in relation to the child (see subsection 64B(3)) may provide for joint or sole decision-making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues.

Section 61DAA of the Act provides the Court with an understanding of what joint decision-making about major long-term issues entails:

61DAA Effect of parenting order that provides for joint decision-making about major long-term issues

(1) If a parenting order provides for joint decision-making by persons in relation to all or specified major long-term issues in relation to a child, then, except to the extent the order otherwise specifies, the order is taken to require each of the persons:

(a) to consult each other person in relation to each such decision; and

(b) to make a genuine effort to come to a joint decision.

(2) To avoid doubt, this section does not require any other person to establish, before acting on a decision about the child communicated by one of those persons, that the decision has been made jointly.

Subject to any order to the contrary, s 61DAB clarifies that if a child is spending time with a person under a parenting order, the order is not taken to require that person to consult with a person who has parental responsibility about decisions that are made in relation to the child that are not major long-term issues.
Because s 60CA and s 60B refer to the best interests of the child, the Court must consider s 60CC which specifies how the Court determines what is in a child’s best interests:

60CC How a court determines what is in a child’s best interests

Determining child's best interests

(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:

(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and

(b) if the child is an Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander child—also consider the matters set out in subsection (3).

General considerations

(2) For the purposes of paragraph (1)(a), the court must consider the following matters:

(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:

(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:

(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

(Emphasis in original)

The new legislative focus is on promoting the safety of children and carers, whereas the former provisions focused on protection from harm. The legislature must have intended there to be a distinction between the former provisions, which focused on protection from harm, and the current provisions which focus on the promotion of safety, otherwise the amendments would lack utility (Melounis & Melounis (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 778). The amendments require a more nuanced, proactive and future-focused approach to considering the safety of children and those who care for them. This will be guided by historical risk assessment.
As stated by Campton J in Barnabas & Phineus [2025] FedCFamC1A 215; (2025) FLC 94-284 at [17]:
The construction of Pt VII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) after the 2024 amendments reinforces the consideration of the safety of children or another person as a focused starting point in those considerations to be weighed in determining a parenting regime that best promotes the best interests of the children (Pierce & Pierce (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1A 162 at [31]).

Emphasis added
Acknowledging this, the Court cautions against lapsing into simplistic binaries such as contending that promoting safety is always different from protection from harm. The reality is much more nuanced. Every case is different. Perhaps in many cases it will make no difference which approach is adopted, but in other cases the nuanced, proactive and future-focused difference is very important to the child and carer.
It should further be noted that due to the repeal of the former s 65DAA of the Act, there is no longer the statutory requirement for the Court consider equal time or substantial and significant time. Cases that refer to this section will need to be carefully considered as to whether they still apply in limited ways, if at all.
The definition of family violence is found in s 4AB of the Act, reproduced below:

4AB Definition of family violence etc.

(1) For the purposes of this Act, family violence means violent, threatening or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family (the family member) or causes the family member to be fearful.

(2) Examples of behaviour that may constitute family violence include (but are not limited to):

(a) an assault; or

(b) a sexual assault or other sexually abusive behaviour; or

(c) stalking; or

(d) repeated derogatory taunts; or

(e) intentionally damaging or destroying property; or

(f) intentionally causing death or injury to an animal; or

(g) unreasonably denying the family member the financial autonomy that he or she would otherwise have had; or

(h) unreasonably withholding financial support needed to meet the reasonable living expenses of the family member, or his or her child, at a time when the family member is entirely or predominantly dependent on the person for financial support; or

(i) preventing the family member from making or keeping connections with his or her family, friends or culture; or

(j) unlawfully depriving the family member, or any member of the family member’s family, of his or her liberty.

(3) For the purposes of this Act, a child is exposed to family violence if the child sees or hears family violence or otherwise experiences the effects of family violence.

(4) Examples of situations that may constitute a child being exposed to family violence include (but are not limited to) the child:

(a) overhearing threats of death or personal injury by a member of the child’s family towards another member of the child’s family; or

(b) seeing or hearing an assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family; or

(c) comforting or providing assistance to a member of the child’s family who has been assaulted by another member of the child’s family; or

(d) cleaning up a site after a member of the child’s family has intentionally damaged property of another member of the child’s family; or

(e) being present when police or ambulance officers attend an incident involving the assault of a member of the child’s family by another member of the child’s family.

The section is both remedial and protective and as such should not be read down by artificial limitations (Project Blue Sky Inc v Australian Broadcasting Authority (1998) 194 CLR 355).
Disputed allegations of family violence must be subjected to the same forensic rigour as any other contested factual issue (Edinger & Duy [2023] FedCFamC1A 194; (2023) 68 Fam LR 55). The party alleging the fact bears the burden of proving it (Wallaby Grip Ltd v QBE Insurance (Australia) Ltd (2010) 240 CLR 444 at [36]) and the standard of the burden is the balance of probabilities (s 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth)). The same point was recently made by the Full Court, though perhaps in less robust terms (Leventis & Leventis [2024] FedCFamC1A 141; (2024) FLC 94-204 at [13]–[19]).

Restraints

In Hartley & Hartley [2021] FedCFamC1F 178 (“Hartley”), McClelland DCJ restated the relevant principles for the granting of injunctions:
The Court is empowered to grant injunctive relief in the nature of that sought by the parties pursuant to s 68B of the Act which relevantly empowers the Court to ‘grant such injunction as it considers appropriate for the welfare of the child’ including, pursuant to s 68B(2), by interlocutory order ‘in any case in which it appears to the court to be just or convenient to do so.’
At the outset it should be noted that the granting of an injunction restraining the exercise of what otherwise would be a person’s inherent rights and freedoms is a serious matter. The breach of such injunction potentially exposes a person to an action for contempt of Court. As noted by the plurality in Cardile v LED Builders Pty Limited [1999] HCA 18 (‘Cardile’) at [31]:
...that the court may grant an injunction in all cases in which it appears to the court to be just and convenient to do so – does not confer an unlimited power to grant injunctive relief. Regard must still be had to the existence of a legal or equitable right which the injunction protects against invasion or threatened invasion, or other unconscientious conduct or exercise of legal or equitable rights.

(references omitted)
In considering the nature of the injunctive relief, it is important to be aware of the general principle that ‘equity intervenes to the minimum extent necessary to do justice’.1
In the context of family law, in Sieling and Sieling [1979] FamCA 23; (1979) FLC 90-627 at 78 264, the Full Court said:

The power to grant injunctions is, of course, a discretionary power, not to be exercised lightly. The Court must balance the hardship to each party of granting or refusing an order and frame its order in such a way to impose no further restriction than is necessary to achieve the protection of the applicant’s interest.
FN1 See Giumelli v Giumelli [1999] HCA 10; (1999) 196 CLR 101 at [10] per Gleeson CJ, McHugh, Gummow, Kirby and Callinan JJ referred to in Norton & Locke [2013] FamCAFC 202; (2013) 284 FLR 51 at [72].

Harmful Proceedings

The recent amendments to the Act have created a robust framework designed to curtail proceedings that are not only unmeritorious and/or vexatious but are harmful. The provisions found in Part XIB of the Act reflect a legislative shift towards providing safeguards to litigants and children from being subjected to the collateral damage that arises due to litigation.
Part XIB of the Act outlines the Court’s powers in dealing with proceedings that are “unmeritorious, harmful, or vexatious” (s 102QAA of the Act). In the Explanatory Memorandum to the Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth) at paragraph [309] (“the Explanatory Memorandum”), the rationale behind Division 1A and 1B of the Act was to “collocate all the Court’s powers for dealing with application or proceedings... to improve usability”.
The harmful proceedings provisions became operative from 6 May 2024. Parliament’s intention behind the harmful proceedings powers is found in the Explanatory Memorandum:
...The purpose of this measure is to protect the respondent and/or children who are the subject of proceedings from the harmful impact of frequent and unnecessary applications filed by an applicant. This measure aims to limit systems abuse, which is a form of family violence that is prevalent in the family law system.
This measure addresses a gap in the court’s powers to scrutinise the institution of further proceedings, cited in the case of Marsden & Winch [2013] FamCAFC 177; (2013) 50 Fam LR 409. The ALRC Report found that the court’s existing vexatious proceedings and summary dismissal powers do not provide sufficient scope for courts to make appropriate orders in cases where one party oppresses the other by repetitive filing of applications and the serving of those applications on the other party.
... The intention of this power is to allow the courts to proactively intervene, or intervene upon application by a party to the proceedings, before further applications are served on the other party, and therefore limit the detrimental effect, major mental distress or psychological harm that may result from further applications.
(Emphasis added).
S 102QAC of the Act provides, as follows:
102QAC Making harmful proceedings orders

Making harmful proceedings orders
A court exercising jurisdiction in proceedings under this Act may make an order (a harmful proceedings order prohibiting a party (the first party) to the proceedings from instituting proceedings under this Act against another party to the proceedings without the leave of the court under section 102QAG, if the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe that:
a) the other party would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party; or

b) in the case of child - related proceedings (within the meaning of Part VII)--the child who is the subject of the proceedings would suffer harm if the first party instituted further proceedings against the other party.

For the purposes of subsection (1), harm may include, but is not limited to, the following:
a) psychological harm or oppression;

b) major mental distress;

c) a detrimental effect on the other party's capacity to care for a child;

d) financial harm.

In determining whether to make an order under subsection (1), the court may have regard to:
a) the history of the proceedings under this Act between the first party and the other party; and

b) whether the first party has frequently instituted or conducted proceedings against the other party in any Australian court or tribunal (including proceedings instituted (or attempted to be instituted) or conducted, and orders made, before the commencement of this section); and

c) the cumulative effect, or any potential cumulative effect, of any harm resulting from the proceedings referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

The court may make a harmful proceedings order on its own initiative or on application by a party to the proceedings.
The court must not make a harmful proceedings order in relation to a person without hearing the person or giving the person an opportunity of being heard.
An order made under subsection (1) is a final order.

Order about notifying other party in relation to application for leave etc.
If the court makes an order under subsection (1), the court must also make an order as to whether the court is to notify the other party, in the event that the first party makes an application under section 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings against the other party, of either or both of the following:
a) that the application has been made;

b) if the application is dismissed—that the application has been dismissed.

The court must have regard to the wishes of the other party in making an order under subsection (7).
A proper understanding of harm in the context of s 102QAC(2) of the Act is found in the non-exclusive, statutory components of harm.

Section 102QAC(2)(a) - psychological harm or oppression

These factors are expressed in the alternate; one or the other may suffice. According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1 ‘harm’ means to “damage or hurt” whilst ‘oppression’ means “the exercise of authority of power in a burdensome, cruel, or unjust manner”. In conjunction, the form of ‘harm’ intended to be addressed by both alternate propositions is limited to purely mental or emotional detriment induced by the imposition, or continuation of, purportedly harmful proceedings oppression. As the definition is inclusive, however, this might mean, for example, that physical and financial oppression is caught by this section.

Section 102QAC(2)(b) - major mental distress

According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1, ‘distress’ means “great pain, anxiety, or sorrow; acute suffering; affliction; trouble”. Mental distress, as a form of harm, is likely to arise from most proceedings under the Act. Justice Behrens noted in Cheadle & Pointer [2025] FedCFamC1F 27 at [84] that “much litigation will cause mental distress” and that “something more is required”. This may be surmised by reference to the statutory inclusion of the presupposition major mental distress in the Act. The form of harm sought to be addressed by this factor is extraordinary distress beyond the norm for the proceeding contemplated. Arguably the harm may be inferred.

Section 102QAC(2)(c) - a detrimental effect on the other party’s capacity to care for a child

Parental capacity is a well-accepted consideration in the determination of parenting orders that does not require explanation here. The inclusion of this factor demonstrates that harm may be indirect in the sense that the proceeding induces a net negative effect on a family unit via the degradation of core competencies of one or more parties. Further, it is important to note that this factor is not limited to adjudication of putative or current parenting proceedings. It may be such that auxiliary, contingent or bifurcated financial proceedings may have a harmful effect on a person’s parental capacity and thus enliven this sub-provision.

Section 102QAC(2)(d) - financial harm

According to the Macquarie Dictionary definition 1 ‘financial’ relates “to monetary receipts and expenditures”. Financial harm must therefore describe the detriment a person suffers through excess or unnecessary pecuniary loss as a result of pending or current proceedings that is beyond the norm for similar claims under the Act.

Commencing property proceedings out of time

Parties have 12-months from the date of a divorce order or a decree of nullity of marriage to commence property proceedings. After this period, any application filed which seeks to commence property proceedings under s 79 of the Act requires an indulgence by way of s 44(3) of the Act, that is, leave to pursue property proceedings out of time. When considering whether to grant leave, the Court must consider the hardship of a party or child to the proceedings that may occur should leave not be granted (s 44(4)(a)). The particulars of s 44 have been reproduced below.
S 44 - Institution of proceedings

(1) Except as otherwise prescribed by the regulations or by the applicable Rules of Court, proceedings under this Act shall be instituted by application.

Proceedings in relation to marriages

(1A) Proceedings under this Act for:
(a) a divorce order in relation to a marriage; or

(b) a decree of nullity of marriage;
may be instituted by either party to the marriage or jointly by both parties to the marriage.

Limitation on applications relating to certain maintenance and property proceedings

(3) Where, whether before or after the commencement of section 21 of the Family Law Amendment Act 1983 :
(a) a divorce order has taken effect; or

(b) a decree of nullity of marriage has been made;
proceedings of a kind referred to in paragraph (c), (caa), (ca) or (cb) of the definition of matrimonial cause in subsection 4(1) (not being proceedings under section 78 or 79A or proceedings seeking the discharge, suspension, revival or variation of an order previously made in proceedings with respect to the maintenance of a party) shall not be instituted, except by leave of the court in which the proceedings are to be instituted or with the consent of both of the parties to the marriage, after the expiration of 12 months after:

(c) in a case referred to in paragraph (a)--the date on which the divorce order took effect; or

(d) in a case referred to in paragraph (b)--the date of the making of the decree.

The court may grant such leave at any time, even if the proceedings have already been instituted.

(3AA) However, if such proceedings are instituted with the consent of both of the parties to the marriage, the court may dismiss the proceedings if it is satisfied that, because the consent was obtained by fraud, duress or unconscionable conduct, allowing the proceedings to continue would amount to a miscarriage of justice.

(3A) Notwithstanding subsection (3), where, whether before or after the commencement of section 21 of the Family Law Amendment Act 1983 :
(a) a divorce order has taken effect or a decree of nullity of marriage has been made; and

(b) the approval under section 87 of a maintenance agreement between the parties to the marriage has been revoked;
proceedings of a kind referred to in paragraph (c), (caa), (ca) or (cb) of the definition of matrimonial cause in subsection 4(1) (not being proceedings under section 78 or 79A or proceedings seeking the discharge, suspension, revival or variation of an order previously made in proceedings with respect to the maintenance of a party) may be instituted:
(c) within the period of 12 months after:
(i) the date on which the divorce order took effect or the date of the making of the decree of nullity, as the case may be; or

(ii) the date on which the approval of the maintenance agreement was revoked;

whichever is the later; or
(d) with the leave of the court in which the proceedings are to be instituted;

and not otherwise.
(3B) Despite subsection (3), if, whether before or after the commencement of Schedule 2 to the Family Law Amendment Act 2000 :
(a) a divorce order has taken effect or a decree of nullity of marriage has been made; and

(b) a financial agreement between the parties to the marriage has been set aside under section 90K or found to be invalid under section 90KA;
proceedings of a kind referred to in paragraph (c), (caa), (ca) or (cb) of the definition of matrimonial cause in subsection 4(1) (not being proceedings under section 78 or 79A or proceedings seeking the discharge, suspension, revival or variation of an order previously made in proceedings with respect to the maintenance of a party) may be instituted:
(c) within the period of 12 months after the later of:
(i) the date on which the divorce order took effect or the date of the making of the decree of nullity, as the case may be; or

(ii) the date on which the financial agreement was set aside, or found to be invalid, as the case may be; or
(d) with the leave of the court in which the proceedings are to be instituted;

and not otherwise.
(4) The court shall not grant leave under subsection (3) or (3A) unless it is satisfied:
(a) that hardship would be caused to a party to the relevant marriage or a child if leave were not granted; or

(b) in the case of proceedings in relation to the maintenance of a party to a marriage--that, at the end of the period within which the proceedings could have been instituted without the leave of the court, the circumstances of the applicant were such that the applicant would have been unable to support himself or herself without an income tested pension, allowance or benefit.
In Whitford & Whitford [1979] FamCA 3; (1979) FLC 90-612 at 78,144 the Full Court held on an application for leave under s 44(3) of the Act:
... two broad questions may arise for determination. The first of these is whether the court is satisfied that hardship would be caused to the applicant or a child of the ... [de facto relationship] if leave were not granted. If the court is not so satisfied, that is the end of the matter. If the court is so satisfied, the second question arises. That is whether in the exercise of its discretion the court should grant or refuse leave to institute proceedings.

ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION

The issues that remain to be determined by the Court are as follows:
How long-term decision-making for the children ought to be allocated;
Who the children ought to live with;
What time, if any, should the children spend with the mother;
The psychological and physical safety of the children in the care of the mother;
Whether the Court should impose restraints on the parents;
Whether the evidence warrants the Court to make an order pursuant to s102QAC of the Act; and
Whether the Court should permit the mother to seek leave pursuant to s 44(3) of the Act, where she has not formally sought such leave.

ISSUES STOOD OVER TO THE FINAL HEARING

Issues arising from the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025

The following orders sought by the mother were stood over to the final hearing arising from her Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025:
That the Court make a finding that the Respondent, [Ms Mavridis], has not been clinically diagnosed with [a mental disorder], and that any prior reference to such diagnosis be disregarded for the purpose of the parenting proceedings.
That the Court direct the Applicant to immediately cease representing to any agency, professional, or individual that the Respondent has [a mental disorder], and that any such past representations be withdrawn in writing.
That the Court make an order prohibiting the use or distribution of any documents or reports that refer to the Respondent has having [a mental disorder], unless supported by independent, recent, and verifiable clinical assessment.
That the Court inform or direct the Applicant to inform all relevant agencies and providers – including: NDIS, [S Family Services] [SS Family Services] DCJ (Department of Communities and Justice) School staff and therapists – that the [mental disorder] diagnosis is not accepted by the Court and is to be retracted from their records where previously relied upon.
That all documents, letters, and reports submitted by the Applicant to external agencies, where the Respondent is described as mentally unstable, violent, or harmful, be disclosed to the Court and struck from the record unless verified.
That the Court issue subpoena’s to all agencies the Applicant has communicated with regarding the Respondent’s mental health or the children’s care, including but not limited to NDIS, [S Family Services], DCJ, and any clinicians or therapists funded through NDIS plans.
That this matter be referred for independent review or case management oversight to ensure procedural fairness and prevent further misuse of mental health allegations or agency processes.
Orders 4–7 sought by the mother are contingent upon findings the Court can only make at a time where the evidence could be tested. To have dismissed or investigated these claims any earlier would have been to unfairly deprive the right of the mother to bring perceived grievances to the Court. Such is the basis for standing these orders over to the final hearing.
Upon review of the material, the Court declines to make order 4 in circumstances where, plainly, a diagnosis of a mental disorder exists and was provided by Dr D as early as 4 May 2020 (Exhibit ICL1). This diagnosis was further supported by Mr T, Clinical Neuropsychologist in his report dated 14 October 2020 (Exhibit A3).
The Court acknowledges that the mother led evidence from a psychological report of Dr O dated 9 November 2024 (“[Dr O’s] Report”) which states at page seven:
Whilst there are clinical similarities and diagnostic overlap between the two disorders, including impairment in interpersonal relationships and emotion regulation challenges, there are differentials that for [the mother] favour a diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder and which supersedes the [mental disorder] diagnosis ... It is important to differ diagnoses in order to prevent misattribution of symptomatology, and to avoid ineffective or management strategies.

(Exhibit R8).
It is clear that there is a difference of clinical opinions between treaters as to the mother’s diagnosis of a mental disorder. However, the Court cannot make a finding that the mother “has not” been diagnosed with a mental disorder and that “that any prior reference to such diagnosis be disregarded for the purpose of the parenting proceedings”, as there is clear evidence that such a diagnosis has been made in the past and it is relevant to the current proceedings. The Court emphasised to the mother that it is not concerned with diagnostic labels but rather is focused on the mother’s behaviour. This focus is seemingly consistent with Dr O’s where he spoke of the importance in distinguishing between the two diagnoses is “to avoid ineffective management strategies” (Exhibit R8). Indeed, the evolution of such a diagnosis is relevant to the proceedings as it provides context surrounding the mother’s behaviour towards the father, the children and to other relevant organisations. The Court declining to make Order 4 sought by the mother, provides a basis for the Court to also decline to make Orders 5–7.
As will be seen in the discussion of the evidence below, Order 8 sought by the mother is wholly inconsistent with the evidence before the Court, and on this basis, the Court declines to make this order.
The Court has informed the mother during previous listings that the Court does not issue subpoenas, and that it is the responsibility of the parties to gather such evidence as they deem appropriate to run their case. The Court declines to make Order 9 sought.
The Court declines to make Order 11 propounded by the mother and has explained to her on past occasions, and during the trial, that she has a right to appeal the decision of the Court should she wish.
For abundant clarity, Orders 4–9 and 11 of the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025 are hereby dismissed.

Issues arising from the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025

The following order sought by the mother was stood over to the final hearing arising from her Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025:
That solicitor [Ms H] be removed from the record as legal representative for the father, [Mr Mavridis], due to serious breaches of legal undertaking, unauthorised dissemination of subpoenaed material, and collusive conduct with the former ICL.
In support of her Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025, the mother filed an accompanying affidavit, however the affidavit was sworn by the mother on 30 July 2025.
The mother contends that Ms H executed an undertaking on 23 May 2025, whereby she stated that she would not “distribute, disseminate, or otherwise share subpoenaed material, including from NSW Police” and then used “that material to influence court orders” (affidavit of the mother filed 4 August 2025, paragraph 42–43). The Court notes that material from sleeve 28 was tendered on behalf of the father during the interim hearing, which is permitted under Rule 6.36(2)(a) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth).
Further, the mother contends that Ms H “provided a copy of [Mr B’s] NSW Police history” to the father and this is evidenced by the father’s affidavit filed 16 June 2025 “where he states that he attended [Suburb U] Police to report [Mr B] for communicating with school staff” (affidavit of the mother filed 4 August 2025, paragraph 98). The contention raised by the mother is incorrect. No evidence was produced by the mother which proves or would provide any basis for concluding that Ms H has supplied the father with a copy of any material produced on subpoena in breach of her undertaking. The father clearly set out in his affidavit filed 16 June 2025 at paragraph 84, that he was advised by the children’s treaters as to the contact made to them by Mr B, and this formed the basis of him making a report to the police.
The mother sent an email to the Independent Children’s Lawyer, my Chambers, the father’s legal representative, and the Chambers of Alstergren CJ on 16 July 2025. Her email further alleged that:
[Ms H] weaponised the subpoena process, unlawfully sharing materials with [the father], enabling him to further abuse the legal system by incorporating fabricated documents and illegally obtained recordings into evidence. She facilitated these actions despite being fully aware they were obtained unlawfully and without my opportunity to object”

(Exhibit A6).
The Court makes clear that Ms H has not, at any point in the proceedings, prohibited the mother from filing a Notice of Objection – Subpoena in relation to any subpoena issued in these proceedings. No other evidence was provided by the mother in support of her claims.
In Mavridis (No 3) at [22] the Court said:
At this stage of the proceedings, the Court declined to remove the father’s solicitor “due to serious breaches of legal undertaking, unauthorised dissemination of subpoenaed material, and collusive conduct with the former ICL” (4 August 2025 Application, Order 3). Despite this, Order 3 sought by the mother was not dismissed on the basis of a possibility that, even though none of the material relied on by the mother suggested any inappropriate action, such material might come to light during the substantive hearing. The Court has not pre-judged the mother’s case and will keep an open mind about her position and contentions.
The mother was asked by the Court, on multiple occasions to provide evidence that substantiated her claims about Ms H. Despite maintaining her assertions about Ms H throughout the entirety of the proceedings, the mother failed to take the Court to any evidence which substantiates her claims in relation to Ms H. Order 3 as contained in the mother’s Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025 is hereby dismissed.

THE EXPERT EVIDENCE

The expert evidence in this matter was prepared by the Expert who prepared an initial Family Report dated 19 September 2023 (“Family Report”) and the Updated Family Report. Both Family Reports are child focused, and deeply sensitive and respectful of the mother. In the Family Report (and in general terms), the recommendation was that the children continue to live with the father who would have sole parental responsibility (Family Report, paragraph 80 and 88) and initially spend supervised time at a contact centre with the mother (Family Report, paragraph 81). It was recommended that the time then progress to being supervised by a maternal family member (Family Report, paragraph 82) and then progress to unsupervised time with the children (Family Report, paragraph 84). By the time of the Updated Family Report, however, the recommendation changed to the children having neither time nor communication with the mother (Updated Family Report, paragraph 48). Moreover, the recommendation was that an order be made preventing the mother from attending the children’s schools, the family home, or any other place where the children may frequent (Updated Family Report, paragraph 50).
The key paragraphs in the Updated Family Report, which explain why the above recommendations were made are numbered 43–47. These paragraphs are reproduced in full in annexure one to these reasons. By way of summary, the recommendations were informed by the following factors:
the vulnerability of all four children due to their neurodivergence and developmental trauma;
the inability of the parents to communicate in any way;
the competing and divergent views of the parents about the children’s needs;
the mother’s profound but unfounded distrust of the children’s treating professionals, and the services that had previously been involved in supporting her contact with the children;
the risk, therefore, that the mother would not cooperate in the children’s treatment, would likely undermine this treatment, and create at best confusion, and at worst distress and regression, for the children;
the assessment formed by the Expert that, based on the mother’s presentation, she is unable to take responsibility for her past behaviours to the extent that there would be any capacity for change;
the possibility that the mother is the source of much of the children’s trauma, and that the court-ordered restitution of supervised contact in 2023 significantly exacerbated the children’s behavioural disturbances at school and at home; and
the father is assessed as being a committed and protective parent who would have been pleased to maintain the relationship between the children and the mother if a safe and productive way could have been identified to achieve this.
The Court makes findings of fact consistent with each of the points set out above. Everything the Expert said is consistent with the totality of the evidence before the Court. Indeed, the Expert’s measured restraint about suggesting that the mother possibly was the source of much of the children’s trauma is the only point of divergence (Updated Family Report, paragraph 45). The totality of the evidence, regrettably, does lead the Court to find that the mother was the source of much of the children’s trauma, and indeed she still presents the greatest risk to their safety and well-being.
The Expert was cross-examined by the mother on 19 November 2025 between 9:51 am and 11:23 am. The mother, unsurprisingly as a layperson, struggled with framing questions appropriately but, in substance and for the most part, they were intelligent questions. The mother challenged the Expert’s impressions that led to her recommendations. The mother posited alternative hypotheses to those of the Expert. She postulated alternative factual scenarios. The Court concludes, however, that nothing arises out of cross-examination that would lead the Court to place less weight on the recommendations of the Expert.
The importance of the following exchange in cross-examination between the mother and the Expert will be discussed below.
Are you aware that autism could also be misrepresented as dysregulation? Did you adjust your assessment of autism, sensory overload, trauma response?---What I was considering was the impact that those kind of behaviours might have had on the children. I wasn’t attributing blame, I was just looking at the impact on the children

I’m talking about their behaviour?---Yes. I absolutely considered that these children’s behaviours are very much in part attributable to their diagnosis.

Because I have been blamed that I caused trauma to the children, whereas autism has been misinterpreted this whole time?---I don’t believe, [Ms Mavridis], that I have blamed you for that. I’ve said that it’s a complex set of factors that have occurred within your family over many years that have resulted in the children presenting as they do.

(Transcript 19 November 2025, p.225 lines 20–32).
The Court has focused on this passage as it in many ways represents a theme of the mother’s case, namely that her neurodivergence explained her dysregulation and, in effect, excused it. An explanation is not always an excuse for conduct. There were several times when the mother appeared before the Court, certainly at the hearing itself but also in earlier Court events and suggested that she was being discriminated against because of her neurodivergence. Speaking in plain terms, it often felt as if the mother was using her neurodivergence as a “get out of jail free” card, or as a “trump” card, making it more likely that she succeeds. A person is generally responsible for their own actions. Context might explain why something occurred but does not necessarily change responsibility.
The response given by the Expert to the first question above reflects the Court’s response to the mother’s neurodivergence. The focus of the Court was never on the mother’s various diagnoses including of neurodivergence, it was on the impact of her behaviours, particularly on her children and those caring for them, irrespective of cause. Those behaviours were demonstrated at several times during the hearing. The transcript alone cannot depict the extent of her dysregulation, how disrupting it was to the case, and how potentially traumatising it may have been to others present.

FINDINGS ABOUT THE CHILDREN’S VULNERABILITIES

Despite the formidable evidence about the children’s vulnerabilities including neurodiversity, the mother remarkably disputed this evidence. This denial was even more surprising given that, on her own evidence, she has not seen the children since her time was suspended on 13 December 2023. As will be seen below, all of the evidence shows that each of the children have made substantial progress with their various diagnoses and have engaged in a variety of different supports. The commitment of the father in ensuring the children can each access the support required by them is a credit to him.

W

W has experienced vulnerabilities with both her mental and physical health. Since March 2020, she has had periods where she has engaged with the Psychological Medicine Department of V Hospital where she attends upon Ms AA, Clinical Psychologist. Her initial presentation, at eleven-years-old included issues relating to incontinence, anxiety, complex trauma, and difficulties with self-care (Exhibit A27). More recently her vulnerabilities consist of Social and Generalised Anxiety Disorder, Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (“PTSD”), and ASD (the father’s affidavit filed 25 July 2025, paragraph 152 (“the father affidavit”)) and depression. Despite her formal diagnosis of ASD, W is not yet been made aware of the same, as the father has opted not to inform her pending guidance from Ms AA (Updated Family Report, paragraph 19).
Ms AA expressed concerns about W in the following manner:
Her anxiety and low mood/depression ... continues to be significantly impacting on her day to day functioning including school, parties and at home” and further that “she has been experiencing panic attacks, gets overwhelmed by noises/having people around her, easily irritated, lack of motivation/meaning, feeling teary/crying and depressed, paralysing fear of people's judgment of her, difficulty sleeping due to worries/negative thoughts, and a 'dissociative feeling' as she describes.

(Exhibit R11).
Ms AA suggested that W have a medication review with Dr BB, Paediatrician to consider the same. During cross-examination, the father advised the Court that W is “doing really well at the moment” and attends upon Ms AA every fortnight (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 29 lines 14–15). Additionally, the father confirmed that W wanted to start an antidepressant medication, that he has supported her choice, and that this has occurred under the recommendations and guidance of Dr BB and Ms AA (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 29 lines 34–41).
W also experiences a connective tissue disorder (Exhibit A26). The father confirmed during cross-examination that W has received funding from the National Disability Insurance Scheme (“NDIS”), and she intends to start physiotherapy and hydrotherapy to aid in the management of this condition (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 29 lines 42–47).

X

X has been diagnosed with episodic low mood and complex trauma (father’s affidavit, paragraph 165). He has attended upon Ms CC of DD Mental Health Services for treatment of episodic low mood, thoughts of self-harm, suicidal ideation, and reduced appetite (Exhibit A32). Recently, as of “the commencement of the school term 2, 2025, [X] had regressed and experienced suicidal ideation and thoughts of self-harming due to not coping with the increased school workload, particularly assignments” (father’s affidavit, paragraph 171). In response, EE School constructed an individual plan for X to implement support for his needs and triggers (Exhibit A33). Currently, X attends upon Ms CC once per fortnight and upon his school psychologist each fortnight in an alternating pattern (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 32 lines 30–35).

Y

Y has been diagnosed with ASD, Oppositional Defiance Disorder (“ODD”), Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (“ADHD”), Complex PTSD, and borderline receptive language and moderate expressive language delay (father’s affidavit, paragraph 179). He receives various supports including occupational and speech therapy, behavioural support intervention, psychiatric and counselling support, and an individual education plan through his school (father’s affidavit, paragraph 180).
In November 2019, the family attended upon FF Family Services for their first family residential stay. During this time Y was observed to become unresponsive to others and engaged in self-harming behaviours. Y reported that he believed he had a devil or demon inside of him when experiencing anger or was overwhelmed (Exhibit A35). The Court notes that the father tendered an audio recording of the mother accompanying the children on the drive to church whereby the mother is heard to say, “be quiet, shut your hell hole, god damn it, devil” (Exhibit 41). The father contends that the mother introduced this “devil” rhetoric to Y, and the Court agrees.
In 2019 during a medical and psychological assessment facilitated through the disability specialist unit of the Sydney Local Health District, it was reported that Y “can be aggressive and impulsive” and “about a year ago, [Y] threw a knife towards his brother” (Exhibit 34). He has been “prescribed [the] medication Intuniv and receives regular in-home support from the Behavioural Support Specialist [Ms N]” (Updated Family Report, paragraph 24). Further, it has become apparent to the father that Y rejects his diagnosis of ASD and has made comments such as “I don’t have autism, I have trauma” and “I want you to get rid of this diagnosis” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 51 lines 18–22). The father informed the Court during cross-examination that presently Y “is very regulated at school”, no longer requires a teachers aid and his relationships with is siblings is “much better” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 49 line 30 to p. 50 line 20).

Z

Z has been diagnosed with ASD, severe ADHD, Complex trauma, severe ODD, severe behavioural issues and difficulties with emotional regulation and learning difficulties (father’s affidavit, paragraph 187). He receives occupational speech therapy, behavioural support, and has an individual education plan (father’s affidavit, paragraph 188). In November 2023, Ms N provided insight into Z’s behaviour where she described that:
... he engages in maladaptive behaviours such as pushing, overturning furniture, throwing furniture or other projectiles, hitting, kicking, spitting, punching others and damaging property. This occurs across all environments and can occur at least once per day, if not multiple times if [Z] is unable to regulate or if he is retriggered. ...These episodes can last up to one hour and [Z] is often unable to fully return to baseline unless he is removed from the area, and he is easily retriggered to crisis point if he remains in the same location as when he was triggered

(Exhibits A18).
During cross-examination, the father advised that Z is currently in a support unit at his school, however, he has progressed in a such a way where he is now able to complete one hour per week in mainstream classes and the school is looking to increase this time (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 51 lines 36–45). Further, the father advised that Z has been able to spend more time with his siblings and he is “much safer at school, and at home, and in the community” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 52 lines 18–20).

FINDINGS ABOUT THE MOTHER INCLUDING ABOUT MENTAL HEALTH AND NEURODIVERSITY

The mother’s own evidence confirms that she has been diagnosed with ASD, Complex–PTSD and Major Depressive Disorder (“MDD”). She forcefully contended that she does not have a mental disorder, and that this “false diagnosis” has been weaponised in this case.
The mother’s contention is inconsistent with the evidence before the Court. The genesis of the diagnosis is a report of her treating psychiatrist, Dr D dated 4 May 2020 and addressed to “NDIS assessors” (“the Report of [Dr D]”) (Exhibit ICL1). This diagnosis was adopted by the mother’s later treating professionals. The diagnosis was the foundation on which the mother received NDIS, and other funding and support, particularly for; but not limited to, the children. On its face, the Report of Dr D states that he had been treating the mother since 6 May 2019, one year before the letter. The clinical notes taken by Dr D also formed part of Exhibit ICL1. At times the mother seemingly asserted that the diagnosis occurred because of the father’s false representations to Dr D, who was a personal friend of the father. She also asserted that the father was present during her consultations with Dr D. The mother’s assertions are inconsistent with the evidence before the Court. The contentious diagnosis of a mental disorder is clearly made after a year of extensive consultation with the mother by her treating psychiatrist.
The Report of Dr D also diagnoses the mother with conditions that she does not cavil with, namely complex-PTSD and MDD. Dr D repeats the diagnosis of a mental disorder in later reports. The diagnosis is consistent with later neuropsychological assessments of the mother dated October 2020 (Exhibit A3). The mother agreed that she was prescribed, and took, medication for a mental disorder for a time. The Court does however, acknowledge that the mother led evidence from Dr O’s Report which opines that the mother “favour[s] a diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder and which supersedes the [mental disorder] diagnosis” (Exhibit R8).
It is not entirely clear to the Court why the mother took such offence, indeed often angry offence, to her diagnosis of a mental disorder. Of its own, such a diagnosis does not preclude good parenting. The focus of the Court in a case about children is not diagnostic labels attributed to a parent, but how the diagnosis might manifest itself in behaviour that has an impact on children. Indeed, the Court reiterated this to the mother and said “[i]’m not interested in labels. I’m interested in the way that people behave... I’m interested in their behaviour and, in particular, do they have the ability to look after their children? So it’s capacity I’m interested in; not the labels.” (Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 132 lines 33–40). The mother’s defensiveness about her diagnosis was unnecessary.

The mother’s inability to regulate her emotions in the Courtroom

The Court records its lay impressions about the mother, based on her manifest behaviour before the Court over a period of five days. The language used here is intended to convey what the Court saw, heard, and experienced. It is not technical language.
The mother frequently said things that led the Court to believe she was paranoid. For example, the mother frequently made assertions that there had been extensive tampering with the evidence adduced by the father in this case or produced on subpoena (Transcript 7 August 2025, p.7 lines 17–23; 17 November 2025, p. 3 lines 23–45; 17 November 2025, p.11 lines 6–11). There is no objective evidence for this. The mother also referred to the collusion between the children’s treating clinicians. Again, there is no objective evidence for this. By no means are these only examples of this.
The mother seemed unable to control her feelings and emotions at times. She frequently raised her voice, leading the Court to form the impression that she was angry, sometimes uncontrollably angry.
She was pervasively unresponsive in cross-examination no matter how often she was instructed to answer the question, or how often she was reminded that she would have the final say in re-examination. Her behaviour continued, even when it was explained to her that it was unhelpful in her case.
The mother was, at times, quite uncontainable. She said what she wanted, when she wanted, ignoring the requests and directions of the Court. She spoke over counsel, witnesses giving evidence, and the Court. A warning that if she failed to comply with the requests and directions of the Court she would be excluded from the courtroom, and the matter would proceed in her absence, elicited cooperation at least for a period.
The mother was unpredictable, and erratic, in her mood and behaviours. There were times when she was the model, courteous, cooperative, advocate in her case but then, seemingly without identifiable trigger, she would erupt into some of the behaviours identified above.
The very experienced Independent Children’s Lawyer in this case made the following comment in closing submissions in relation to the mother’s behaviour in Court:
