...one of the remarkable aspects of this case, and I have to say I’ve been practising for close to 40 years and I haven’t seen behaviour like that that has been exhibited by [Ms Mavridis] before in the witness box, and I’ve spent a lot of my time in court. I would say your Honour would have regard to the behaviour that you have actually seen. Now whether that behaviour is attributed to autism spectrum disorder, whether that’s attributed to some other disorder, or whether it’s attributable to no disorder at all, your Honour would be concerned at that behaviour.

(Transcript 19 November 2025, p. 271, lines 21–28).
The Court agrees. All of these behaviours may be summarised using the relatively neutral term of dysregulation. It was disconcerting to the Court that the mother’s dysregulation continued even after the Court openly explained to her that the Court was concerned that if she behaved in this fashion in a public courtroom, before the person who would make an important decision about her future relationship with her children, then it might also be concerned about how she would behave in a private context, including when interacting with her four vulnerable children. This did not deter the mother. Impression management is an issue that every trial judge must be conscious of when hearing evidence. This was not an issue with the mother’s evidence. It was unfiltered and raw. Despite being given the opportunity to demonstrate a capacity for emotional self-regulation for even a moderate period, the mother was unable to do so.

The mother’s inability to regulate her emotions in front of the children in the home environment

During the course of the proceedings the father tendered four audio recordings of the mother in the company of one or more of the children (Exhibit A41). It is not necessary for the Court to comment on each of the videos, however, the Court notes that the mother’s dysregulation is present in all four videos. The mother contended that these audio recordings had “been modified or edited” and that the father “does not have a single person that can corroborate any of his stories” (mother’s affidavit filed 17 June 2025, paragraph 98–92). The Court rejects the contentions raised by the mother, where it observed first-hand the types of dysregulation and outbursts the mother can experience, which were consistent with those heard in the audio recordings.

Audio file entitled “[Ms Mavridis] with [Z]” taken on 18 July 2019(Exhibit A41)

During this audio recording, the mother is heard to get progressively dysregulated in the company of Z, who was two years old at the time. The mother appears to become overwhelmed by the consistent demands of raising four young children. She would respond to the children’s demands for attention by raising her voice and changing her tone to incorporate aggression. During the audio file, there are times when the mother will say something in response to the children with aggression before restoring herself to a level of composure. However, when the mother becomes triggered, by no identifiable trigger, she becomes repetitive, hostile, and inappropriate. Whilst there was no independent transcript of the audio recordings available to the Court, the Court was able to hear the mother say the following statements when listening to the exhibit during the trial and further re-listening to the exhibit in Chambers:
“Oh my god you little shit now I going to fucking lose my fucking head this is not on...you are naughty give me a break go to bed... I can’t handle this you are doing my head in”;
“Damn me to hell... damn me to hell. I’m sick of the pain every god damn day”;
“Damn me to hell, I hate my life, damn me to hell. Go to bed goddammit I can’t take it anymore give me a fucking break...damn me to hell”;
“Stay in there all day for all I care I have had enough... damn me to hell... damn me to Satan... send me to hell...just go to sleep and give me a fuckin break...damn me to hell...send me to Satan”; and
“Oh fucking stab me in the eye...I hate myself...go to sleep...send me to Satan”

(Exhibit A41).

When this video was played in Court, the mother commented that the audio was recorded at “a moment of high stress and anxiety trying to get a house ready, and get it cleaned, get dinner ready” (Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 169 lines 3–4). The Court accepts the mother’s contention, which provides context for, but it does not excuse her conduct. Indeed, during the audio recording the mother can be heard speaking with someone on the phone where she asks what time she should have dinner prepared by. The mother fails to recognise the inappropriateness of her behaviour and the impact that it can have on the children. The mother consistently referenced “hell” and the “devil”, which is a theme that has been recurrent for Y during his own episodes of dysregulation.

Audio file entitled “[Ms Mavridis] with [Y] & [X]” taken on 13 October 2019 (Exhibit A41)

During this audio recording, more than one of the children appear to be screaming and crying. The Court can hear one of the children say, “you are going to kill me, you are going to kill me, I don’t want you to smack me”. The father contends that it is Y, who said this, and the Court does not cavil with this contention. Further, the father suggests that Y resorted to hiding under the bed during this exchange with is mother. The Court notes that this is supported by the mother saying, “get out or you will get another one, get out or you will get another smack” (Exhibit A41). The reference to “get out” suggests that Y positioned himself in such a way that the mother cannot get to him, such as, getting under the bed. Acts of aggression
The father details two incidents whereby the mother hit glass in response to her dysregulation. Firstly, on Mother’s Day in 2016, the father alleged that the mother admitted to punching the glass windowpane at the front door (father’s affidavit, paragraph 226–227). Secondly, on 28 June 2018, the father alleged that the mother admitted to punching the glass sliding door in response to being angry with Y, causing the door to crack (father’s affidavit, paragraph 247). More generally, the father alleged that “on a weekly basis between 2017 until she moved out in 2019, there were many times when [he] personally witnessed and heard [the mother] punch different walls of the house and the kitchen benches around the children” (father’s affidavit, paragraph 234).
During cross-examination, the mother admits to smashing “a little panel at the front door” (Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 156 lines 44–45), however, the Court cannot ascertain when the incident conceded by the mother occurred. The mother contends that this incident did not occur in front of the children. However, after further questions from counsel, the mother provided the following context:
My daughter was having extreme meltdown at the front of the house inside, and I had the other three in the car, and they were at the front of the house. So they – I was anxious about them being in the car, and my daughter screaming, and I can’t be in two places at once. And I admit I had a meltdown. Didn’t know what to do, because I can’t be two places at once so.

(Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 157 lines 6–11).
Noting that, the mother conceded smashing a “panel at the front door”, and that W was having a melt down at the front of the house, the Court finds on the balance of probabilities, that this incident occurred in front of at least W.
The mother also conceded that she “used to bang the wall” in response to having a “meltdown”, however she insists that her behaviour was “not violence” but rather it was “undiagnosed and not knowing of [her] autism” (Transcript 8 August 20925, p. 157 lines 14–19). Even if the mother did not know that she had ASD, she must have been aware of her propensity for anger and history of verbally and physically lashing out.
The Court was taken to Exhibit A5 which is an outpatient initial assessment record completed by GG Hospital in March 2020. The record relates to the mother and records that, she “bangs walls out of frustration”, in “August 2019 – issues @ home. I broke a glass door with my hands”, and “getting kids ready for school + afternoons – their behaviour caused her to scream ‘I hate you’”. This self-reported history from the mother supports the contentions raised by the father, that the mother exhibits unregulated behaviour in the presence of the children.

Unsuccessful supervised time between the mother and the children

On 3 November 2022, Senior Judicial Registrar Hayward ordered that the mother and children spend three hours each fortnight together, supervised by HH Contact Centre which the Court understands to be a professional supervision service provider. Initially, both parents reported that the supervised visits “were going well” (Family Report, paragraph 49). The father stated that HH Contact Centre “are using a more engaged model of facilitated contact, rather than mere supervision, which he said has the benefit of role modelling desirable parenting behaviours for [the mother], so that both she and the children derive greater benefit from their contact time” (Family Report, paragraph 49) (Emphasis in original). However, as is seen from the evidence before the Court, the children’s behaviour significantly regressed during their supervised time with the mother.
Despite claiming that “the documents are fake” in relation to the material produced under subpoena sleeve two from HH Contact Centre, the mother sought to tender the entirety of the material produced (Transcript 19 November 2025, p. 257 line 38 to p. 258 line 36) (Exhibit R10). The mother merely made generalised statements as to the document’s authenticity and accuracy and did not take the Court to any information to support her claim. In these circumstances, the Court rejects the mother’s contention that the material is “fake” and treats the document as a business-like record.
The Court accepts that the time the mother spent with the children supervised by HH Contact Centre was limited. However, it is evident that their behaviour was consistently challenging and was unable to be appropriately managed by the mother. In an observation report dated 2 September 2023, it was recorded that:
[Z]... picks up a children’s plastic chair, grunting and throwing it towards [Y]. As [the mother] attempts to hold [Z] back by holding onto his shirt, [Z] yells out to [the mother] that she’s a ‘stupid bitch’. When questioned from [the mother] where he got the language from, [Z] responded ‘Mum’

(Exhibit R10).
It was recorded that it was, in fact, the contact worker who intervened and redirected Z’s behaviour. Further, there was an instance where the mother and children were discussing words in Country JJ language, and Z “smiles and spells out ‘F.U.C.K’, followed by saying ‘Fuck’”. It is recorded that the mother “turned to worker two saying ‘I can't discipline that’”. Later in the session Z “picked up chairs and toys to throw and swore at worker one and worker two stating ‘fuck you’, ‘you son of a bitch’, ‘you’re a bitch’. Z also attempted to hit worker one above the shoulders several times”, thereafter Z and Y began trying to hurt each other. During this time, the mother remained “playing ball” with W and X (Exhibit R10).
Similar behaviour was displayed during a contact visit in September 2023. During this time, Z and Y were throwing objects at each other and began to fight each other. It was recorded that the mother:
was not focused much on managing [Y] and [Z’s] behaviour. When the mother did attempt to intervene, it was recorded that “[Z] would shout and swear at [the mother] and would say ‘Get off you me you fucking bitch, you make him hit me. Don't hold me’. On the other hand [Y] would say, ‘I hate you white pig, don't like you’ referring to [Z]”.

(Exhibit R10).
The HH Contact Centre workers commented that when Z and Y would fight, the mother “was observed to remove herself from the situation and would either sit down with [W] or start picking up items that were thrown on the floor” (Exhibit R10).
On 18 September 2023, Ms KK, Coordinator of HH Contact Centre emailed the Independent Children’s Lawyer expressing concerns regarding the children’s behaviour during their supervised sessions. Ms KK, expressed:
The CCS has provided additional support to the staff and attempted to implement a range of positive strategies to support the children during their visits. Unfortunately, we continue to observe a range of high‐risk behaviours including violent/assaultive behaviours towards each other, [the mother] and the staff, absconding, property destruction and verbal abuse (particularly high level swearing and name calling). We have seen little evidence of [the mother] successfully implementing the behaviour support strategies, or attempting to soothe or regulate the children when they are escalating. Furthermore, [the father] (father and lived with parent) has reported on many occasions that the behaviours continue after leaving the centre. These behaviours are of grave concern as they include self‐harming, physical assaults and throwing items at each other, while the father is trying to concentrate on driving the children home.

(Exhibit A16).
When meeting with the Expert to prepare the Updated Family Report, the mother advised that “the visits had stopped because [HH Contact Centre] was not equipped to deal with neurodivergent children”. Additionally, the mother contended that “[HH Contact Centre’s] decision to suspend the visits was at the behest of the ICL and asserted that all decisions had been made on the basis of ‘it being too difficult for [the father]’... with no regard for the children’s need for a relationship with their mother” (Updated Family Report, paragraph 38).
It is apparent from the material before the Court, that HH Contact Centre invested various resources to attempt to help facilitate the time between the mother and the children. The Court has no reason to doubt the suggestion by Ms N that the service would also select staff members who were familiar to the family and brought in external staff members who have knowledge of children with disabilities (Report of Ms N dated 23 October 2023, page 4). In September 2023, Ms N held a joint training session for both the mother and HH Contact Centre staff, and on 7 October 2023, HH Contact Centre reduced the visits from four children to two children per visit to attempt to make the visits more manageable. The training session delivered by Ms N explored:
known triggers for [Y] and [Z], support strategies, reactive strategies and emphasised the boys’ known triggers, such as being ‘touched’. It was interactive with videos reinforcing key elements and to provide different visual stimuli or ways of explaining concepts to invite [the mother] to participate

(Report of [Ms N] dated 23 October 2023, page 4).
During such training, the mother was observed to “disengaged from the training session and seemed unable to or became unwilling to increase her capacity as a parent” (Report of Ms N dated 23 October 2023).
The mother’s lack of insight into her own parenting deficits, particularly in relation to her ability to manage the extreme behaviours of the children is of great concern to the Court, especially where Z and Y consistently engaged in behaviour which increased the likelihood of physical injury to themselves and those around them.

The children’s regression following supervised contact with the mother

S Family Services commenced working with this family in August 2020 and assisted with a variety of therapeutic interventions. It appears that the service would occasionally facilitate phone calls between the mother and the children and worked with the father and the children on a more regular basis to assist with regulation. Ms P observed in her report dated November 2023 at page 7, that “consistent time away from [the mother] saw great improvements on all aspects of family functioning”, and further that it is predicted that future contact with the mother would “lead to the children’s behaviour regressing due to exposing them to ongoing psychological harm”. Additionally, Ms P commented that “contact sessions are chaotic and unsettled, requiring workers to continuously step in as [the mother] is unable to provide boundaries, nurture or guidance” (Report of Ms P dated November 2023, page 7).
During the period of time the children engaged in supervised contact with the mother through HH Contact Centre in 2023, each of the children regressed. Particular regression was experienced by Z, who’s time with the mother eventually ceased by consent on 8 November 2023, as a result of his behaviours. A letter dated November 2023 from LL School addressed to Z’s treaters outlines that throughout semester two he has “hit various students 22 times”, refused “to complete therapy and is aggressive towards therapists”, “thrown furniture and other items at staff 10 times”, and “destroyed school property and the class room 10 times” (Exhibit A17). It is apparent that the school attempted to implement a variety of strategies to aid in improving Z’s behaviour, however it was noted that his “behaviour is still compromising the learning and safety of himself and others” (Exhibit A17).
Ms N’s report dated 23 October 2023 made the following observations of each of the children’s regressions since re-engaging in supervised contact with the mother:
W reported feeling of anxiety relating to comments made by the mother and since June 2023 W begun “putting herself in harm’s way to stop her younger siblings from hurting each other”;
X has recommenced using language that his mother regularly used with his siblings, such as calling Y a “demon, retard, devil”. Particularly after contact visits he “regularly withdraws, isolating himself further”; and
Y started to become disengaged with his Speech Pathologist and Occupational Therapist. He also reengaged in self-harming behaviours directly after a contact session and required the father to pull over and help regulate his emotions. Further, Y begun recalling negative memories associated with the mother, having nightmares and exhibiting aggressive behaviour.
During cross-examination Ms N maintained that “every time I’ve been at the house, it has been a happy household, other than the very traumatic regression period in 2023” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 79 lines 4–6). It was put to Ms N that the children’s upward trajectory could have been due to the success of their medication and not purely to the absence of contact with the mother. In response, Ms N detailed a time where Z was held in an “unauthorised hold” by a support worker, and in a similar presentation to how the mother held him during a contact visit, and it caused Z to regress for a period of two-weeks and have an outburst at school. During such incident, Z was medicated “so any triggers relating to their trauma significantly regress their behaviour” (Transcript 7 August 2025, p. 79 line 35 to p. 80 line 5). The opinion of Ms N in relation to the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of the children’s medication for moderating the children’s triggers has not been given any weight by the Court as it is not within her area of expertise.
The Court accepts the observations of Ms N relating to the children’s regression. Her observations are consistent with the behaviour’s observed in the HH Contact Centre subpoena material (Exhibit R10), and independent material of the children’s treaters. Additionally, as outlined earlier in these reasons, the children’s current status shows significant improvement during a period of time they have not spent any time with the mother.

The mother’s inability to keep the children safe and free from harm

The inability of the mother to regulate her emotions is particularly problematic from the perspective of the safety of the children. The first finding the Court makes in this regard is that it is highly unlikely that the mother will behave differently with the children, then she did in Court. The evidence of the inability of the mother to regulate her behaviour with the children in the past is noted above. The impact on them is likewise noted. The second finding the Court makes is that the children’s own diagnoses make them especially vulnerable to the mother’s dysregulation. One of the challenging behaviours of the children is itself dysregulation.
The Court also finds that the mother’s inability to prioritise the children’s safety is evidenced by the evidence she gave about her partner, Mr B. The mother commenced a relationship with Mr B in “mid-2023” (Updated Family Report, paragraph 5), however, in cross-examination on 17 November 2025 she explained that she was “not on speaking terms at the moment” with him, thus inferring they were no longer in a relationship (Transcript 17 November 2025, p. 17 lines 42–43). Nonetheless, he was someone who has personally helped the mother in the course of this case. The mother denies the criminality of Mr B and, indeed, asserts that the documents produced by the New South Wales Police in subpoena sleeve 28 evidencing this are fabricated and manufactured. In the alternative, she seemed to contend that his criminal record was irrelevant to the Court case. Indeed, the COPS entries about him are “ridiculous” (Transcript 17 November 2025, p. 59 line 11). The Court will deal with this contention shortly. She contends that he is a “great guy” (Transcript 17 November 2025, p.54 lines 14–18). Indeed, she told the Court in cross-examination that he was a “great father... someone that really loved his children”, and she would have no concern about him helping the mother care for the children (Transcript 17 November 2025, p.56 lines 34–41).
The evidence of the criminal history of Mr B is found in Exhibit A7 which is a document produced by New South Wales Police pursuant to a subpoena. Despite the assertions of the mother, there is no evidence to suggest this as anything other than what it appears, namely an authentic document which constitutes a business record of New South Wales Police.
Exhibit A7 reveals the following convictions of Mr B:
In mid-2016, the City MM District Court found Mr B guilty of a count of “stalk/intimidate intend fear physical etc harm (domestic) T2”. However, the Court utilised s 10 of the Crimes (Sentencing Procedure) Act 1999 (NSW) (“Crimes Act”) and required Mr B to participate in an anger management and counselling program for a period of two years;
In mid-2020, the Suburb NN Local Court heard a “detention application notice after breach etc”. The Court found that a breach of bail had been established;
In late 2022, the Suburb OO Local Court heard a “detention application notice after breach etc”. The Court found that a breach of bail had been established; and
In early 2023, the City MM District Court found Mr B guilty of the following:
A count of “use carriage service to menace/harass/ offend”, and imposed a $500 fine and subjected him to three years of supervision;
Multiple counts of “contravene prohibition/ restriction in AVO (Domestic)” and imposed a 12-month aggregate sentence of imprisonment;
Multiple counts of “stalk/intimidate intend fear physical etc harm (domestic) – T2” and imposed a 12-month aggregate sentence of imprisonment; and
“Contravene prohibition/restriction in AVO (domestic) and imposed a 7-month sentence of imprisonment.
It is apparent from the summary above that Mr B has engaged in physical and virtual fear inducing behaviour in a past relationship and is not deterred by Court imposed restrictions.
Mr B is referred to in the Updated Family Report. The mother explains that she met Mr B in mid-2023 through an online platform, but they do not cohabit. She described him as “...her most significant source of support at the present time.” (Updated Family Report, paragraph 39). Paragraph 39 of the Updated Family Report further states:
She said she is aware that [Mr B] has a criminal record and that some of his charges relate to domestic violence against previous partners, but she said that their relationship is not a violent one. She held the view that [Mr B] has been similarly wronged by his ex-wife to the way in which she considers she has been wronged by [Mr Mavridis]. She reported that he has been “alienated” from his three children, and that he, like her, experiences complex PTSD related to his experiences of being falsely accused and separated from his children.
The father reported to the Expert that he is fearful of Mr B (Updated Family Report, paragraph 33).
At paragraph 44 of the Updated Family Report, the Expert opines that the mother may have formed a “trauma bond with [Mr B], ...in which they unhelpfully reinforce each other’s perceptions of reality, preventing any possibility of personal responsibility for change”. This is a plausible opinion which the Court accepts. The Court notes that one possible example of the unreal perceptions referred to by the Expert is the mother’s denial of the serious criminal record of Mr B, and of his suitability to assist in the care of the children.
As early as 11 April 2023, the Court has indicated to the mother that Mr B “comments [were not] helpful or conducive to the conduct of the proceedings” (Orders 11 April 2023, Notation C and E). This was reiterated in Mavridis at [43] where the Court said it was likely that “[Mr B] is a significant hindrance to her case” and that it is possible that the mother is “being taken advantage of by [Mr B], for unknown reasons”. Yet, four days before the commencement of the final hearing the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding attempting to have Mr B “reinstated” as her “support person with immediate effect, with full permission to assist her in all legal preparation, attend future court hearings, communicate with service providers, and have access to relevant documents as permitted under Rule 11.03...” (Order 9 sought in the Application in a Proceeding dated 4 August 2025).
The Court finds that Mr B is a threat to the safety of the children, particularly if they were left in his care, or he was asked to support the mother in her care of the children. The mother is a threat to the safety of the children in believing that Mr B would be a suitable carer for the children, in any capacity. She is oblivious about both dangers.

UNACCEPTABLE RISK OF HARM

In Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 (“Isles & Nelissen”), the Full Court of this Court (Alstergren CJ, McClelland DCJ, Aldridge, Austin, and Tree JJ) reconsidered the issue of unacceptable risk. In the course of their reasons, their Honours observed that unacceptable risk is not to be established on the balance of probabilities.
Simply put, the Court is required to make factual findings about past relevant disputed events underpinning the allegations, to the requisite standard of proof and then on the basis of those findings and other relevant considerations, predict the level of future risk to a child. In carrying out the risk assessment, the Court assesses both the prediction of future harm and the severity of the impact, if such harm were to eventuate. The possibility of future harm and severity of harm may be determined as unacceptable.
In Eastley & Eastley [2022] FedCFamC1A 101; (2022) FLC 94-094 (“Eastley”), the Full Court observed that a trial judge must consider the cumulative effect of the whole of the evidence to assess the level of risk posed to a child, rather than dealing with individual events in isolation at [33]:
The first point to be made is that the law did not require the primary judge to assess the potency of the risk of harm posed to the children by reference to the evidence concerning individual events in isolation from the remainder of the evidence. On the contrary, the primary judge was required to assess the level of risk posed to the children on the whole of the evidence, since the strength of the evidence lies in its cumulative effect, much like how the strength of rope derives from the combination of its individually weaker strands (Transport Industries Insurance Co Ltd v Longmuir [1997] 1 VR 125 at 127–130 and 141; Savage v Lunn [1998] NSWCA 203; J.D Heydon, Cross on Evidence (LexisNexis Australia, 13th edition, 2021) at [1110] and [9040]).
Having made the findings that the Court does above, on the basis of the whole of the evidence, the Court concludes that the mother presents an unacceptable risk to the safety of the children that cannot be ameliorated in any acceptable manner. This, naturally, influences the discussion of what orders are to be made in the best interests of the children.

DISCUSSION ABOUT BEST INTERESTS

It is self-evident that this Court must make orders that are in the best interests of the children. In order to achieve this the Court must make orders that promote the safety of the children including from being subjected or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm. The safety of the carer of the children must also be promoted.
The Court is satisfied that there are multiple future risks to the safety of the children if they were to spend time or communicate with the mother. The evidence in this case leads the Court to be comfortably satisfied that the mother’s inability to regulate her behaviour, including her emotions, is the greatest risk to the safety of her children. The evidence demonstrates how, even when supervised contact was tried, it had to be suspended because of the mother’s behaviour. The mother’s inability to regulate her behaviour also explains why she should not be allowed to communicate with the children. Whilst these reasons attempt to depict the dysfunctional nature of the mother’s communication in Court, there is ample evidence before the Court of her equally dysfunctional written communication (Exhibit A6; Exhibit A24). The safety risks of the children are magnified by their own experience of neurodivergence and or mental health diagnoses.
Further, it is not safe for the children to spend time or communicate with the mother because of her relationship with Mr B, and her belief that he is a suitable carer for the children. The evidence clearly indicates that he is not, and the failure of the mother to perceive this merely accentuates the safety issues associated with her spending time and communicating with the children.
The Court recognises that the father’s case included other reasons for the making of the orders made in this case. His evidence alleges the family violence towards him in the presence of the children, and towards the children. Without in any way minimising this violence, and its impact on both the father and the children, it was not necessary to consider this evidence and to make findings, in order to make orders in the best interests of the children. Let there be no doubt, however, that whilst findings will not be made, the totality of the evidence about the violence perpetrated by the mother would lead the Court to a strong impression that she perpetrated the same.
In this case the children have expressed views about not spending time with the mother, but the Court has chosen not to rely on this evidence both because it was unnecessary, and because it could inadvertently, and at some future time, cause the children to believe that they influenced the decision of this Court.
The Court must consider the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the children. As both parents are of Country JJ cultural background, the Court is confident that the father will expose the children to their Country JJ heritage as he considers appropriate. He is also far better equipped and has demonstrated himself to be more than equal to what must be at times the onerous task of meeting the remaining needs of the children. For now, the greatest need of the children is for the father to be free to focus on those diverse needs and free from the distraction of the present proceedings.
The issues and evidence that have been considered in these reasons for judgement as warranting the orders that have been made clearly signal the abundant capacity of the father, and the inadequate capacity of the mother, to not just provide for the needs of the children, but to prioritise them over their own personal needs. One of the most significant aspects of parental capacity is insight, for example in relation to the effect of their actions and omissions on the children. One of the saddest aspects of this case was the depth of the mother’s lack of insight into the needs of her children. Her behavioural dysregulation, her blind faith in Mr B, the manner in which she conducted this litigation including her paranoia and aggression, all signal a deep failure to understand the broader impacts of this on her own children, by distracting the father and diminishing his ability to parent.
There can be no doubt that the children would benefit from having a relationship with the mother, as once they did. But it must be safe to do so, and it clearly is not, at least not at this time. As the mother lacks insight about her own behaviour, it is hard to foresee that she might access professional services to address, and reverse, the underlying causes of her behaviour. The mother’s previous treating psychologist, Mr PP provided a report dated 14 March 2023, where he said:
While [the mother’s] diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder may contribute to her reported behaviours, it is important to note that this does not excuse or justify any harmful actions towards her children or others. Rather, it underscores the importance of understanding the underlying factors contributing to her behaviours in order to provide appropriate interventions and support.

(Exhibit A4).
The Court accepts this evidence. However, the mother has shown that she is unable to depart from her thinking that her behaviours are merely a product of her ASD and is unable to consider the effect this has had on the children. The mother contended that her ASD diagnosis “provides [a] clinical explanation for distress responses misinterpreted as aggression” and “demonstrates the [mother] required accommodations never provided” (mother’s written submissions, page 3). The evidence before the Court highlights the variety of services the mother has attended upon to attempt to build her parental capacity, such as the following: QQ Family Services in 2017, RR Family Services and SS Family Services in 2018, TT Health Services in 2019, S Family Services in 2020, and HH Contact Centre in 2023 (Report of Ms N dated 23 October 2023). Regrettably, even where appropriate interventions and support have been implemented, the mother has been unable to develop her parental capacity such that she is able to safely care for the children. Indeed, the case ran by the mother was fixated on the ways in which she perceives herself to be a victim of the system and did not focus on the needs of the children, or how the children and herself would cope should the Court make the proposal she sought.
In the circumstances of this case the father should have the unfettered ability to make both long-term and short-term decisions about the children without having to consult with the mother. Consultation would be traumatic for him and distracting in terms of his parenting capacity.

RESTRAINTS

The father sought that the mother be restrained by injunction from contacting him or the children, from coming within 100 metres of the former matrimonial home, or where the family may reside, work or attend, and from posting material in relation to the father, children or proceedings on social media (father’s orders sought, order 4). The mother sought similar restraints be made against the father (mother’s orders sought, order 8). The mother’s evidence did not address why she sought such restraints. Regardless, the orders which the Court has made in relation to decision making authority and who the children will live with, make the restraints sought by the mother null and void.
At paragraph 125 of the father’s affidavit, he states that he:
... hold[s] serious concerns about the possibility of [the mother] and her partner attending the family home and escalating in front of the children if there are no orders restraining [the mother] coming to the family home or attending the children’s schools, my place of work or where we frequent.
Indeed, there appears to be a history of the mother attending the former matrimonial home unannounced and uninvited. Ms N stated that previously “[X] was re-exposed to ... triggers when his mother would turn up at the house and his school without warning and attempt to see him and his siblings” (Report of Ms N dated 23 October 2023, page 2). Additionally, Ms P, Family Counsellor noted that the mother’s sporadic contact and engagement with the children was “destabilising and confusing”, when she ceased engaging with in sessions with the children through S Family Services, however “she was arriving unannounced at the school and home and countered the safety plans we had put in place” (Report of Ms P dated November 2023, page 2). Further, in an email to the mother dated 24 March 2021 from Ms UU and Ms P of S Family Services, it is recorded that the mother continued to contact the father despite being asked not to from both the father and S Family Services (Exhibit A10).
The mother tendered three emails and various attachments sent by her to Chambers at 12:15 am, 12:26 am, and 12:34 am on 17 November 2025 (Exhibit R2). Within the attachments are screenshots of a conversation between the mother and an unknown Instagram user by the username “[...]”. The unknown user states “... if you truly love your children you will stop airing all this on social media” (Exhibit R2). This statement highlights that the mother has posted about the family situation on social media in the past. The Court also notes that the mother has previously consented to a restraint from “posting any comments or material whatsoever in relation to these proceedings on any form of social media” (Order 8 of the orders made 3 November 2022). Naturally, any such post regarding the proceedings which identifies a party to the proceedings potentially offends s 114Q of the Act.
The Court has a wide discretion under s 68B, which includes orders in the form of an injunction for the personal protection of a parent (s 68B(1)(b)(i)). Although an injunction under s 68B is not a “parenting order” as defined by s 64B of the Act (Hedlund & Hedlund [2021] FedCFamC1A 84; (2021) FLC 94-065 at [118]), the Court may nevertheless give careful consideration to the paramountcy principle where the orders sought to be made intimately concern the welfare of the children. This was confirmed by the Full Court in Bennett v Bennett [2001] FamCA 462; (2001) FLC 93-088 (See Attwood v Attwood [2022] FedCFamC1F 6 at [32]). The Court may grant injunctions under the Act where it appears to be “justice or convenient to do so” (s 68B(2)) However, it must exercise such power “lightly” and “balance the hardship to each party of granting or refusing an order” (Hartley at [14]).
Based on the evidence before the Court, and the findings made regarding the mother’s limited insight and capacity to keep the children free from harm, previous conduct and the possibility of future harm, the Court endorses the restraints sought by the father. The injunctions sought by the father are framed in such a way to impose no further restriction than is necessary for his and the children’s protection. Restraining the mother from making contact with the father or children, from attending their home, school or workplace, and from posting on social media about them all act as a protective measure to ensure their therapeutic progress is not disturbed.
The Court has, on its own motion, included a further restraint on the mother such that she may not direct, cause, encourage or facilitate any other person from doing anything to which she herself is restrained from. The Court considers this additional restraint appropriate in circumstances where; the mother has previously requested Mr B to complete various tasks on her behalf such as, respond to emails to the father’s legal representatives, contact the children’s treating professionals, and to send a letter to Chambers (the mother’s affidavit filed 4 August 2025, paragraphs 66, 71, 73). The Court reiterates that the children’s therapeutic progress must be protected.

HARMFUL PROCEEDINGS ORDER

The father sought that the Court make an order pursuant to s102QAC(1) of the Act such that the mother be restrained from instituting further proceedings without permission of the Court (father’s orders sought, order 5). Both subsections of s 102QAC(1) are relevant in this matter, such that the Court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds to believe both the father and the children would suffer harm if the mother was to institute further proceedings in this Court against the father.

Summary of procedural history

The Court has explored the procedural history of this matter earlier in these reasons, and will not repeat the history verbatim here. However, during the course of the proceedings, the mother has brought the following applications:
Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025;
This Application in a Proceeding sought, amongst other things, to discharge the Independent Children’s Lawyer and to have a new Independent Children’s Lawyer appointed, for the Court to refer the father for fraud, for the Court to make a finding that the mother has not been clinically diagnosed with a mental disorder, for her time with the children to be immediately reinstated and for the matter to be referred for independent review or case management oversight.
Application in a Proceeding filed 7 May 2025;
This Application in a Proceeding sought that the former matrimonial home be sold and the proceeds be held in trust. Additionally, the mother sought that the net proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home be split 70/30 in her favour.
Oral application to adduce further evidence on 24 June 2025 in relation to the interim hearing held on 20 June 2025.
Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025;
This Applications in a Proceeding sought that I recuse myself, for the father’s solicitor to be removed from the proceedings, and for the previous Independent Children’s Lawyer to be reviewed. Additionally, the mother sought for her time with the children to be reinstated, for Mr B to be reinstated as her support person, and that she be granted leave to file her application to commence property proceedings (“the mother’s recusal application”).
Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025;
This Application in a Proceeding filed by the mother sought a stay of some of my orders from 20 June 2025, which related to the reserving of my judgment over a previous application filed by the mother and the restraint relating to Mr B (“the mother’s stay application”).
Application in a Proceeding filed 8 August 2025.
This Application in a Proceeding sought, again, that I recuse myself (“the mother’s second recusal application”).
I have issued the following judgments in relation to the above applications:
Mavridis;
Mavridis (No 2); and
Mavridis (No 3).
The Court notes that the mother’s oral application to adduce further evidence on 24 June 2025 in relation to the interim hearing held on 20 June 2025 which is detailed in Mavridis (No 2), was an application made with no prospect of success. It failed to interact with any relevant points of law for such application and resulted in the application being dismissed. The mother’s conduct of bringing litigation that is without merit, was repeated and detailed in Mavridis (No 3). As explained in Mavridis (No 3), the mother did not “address the [legal] requirements” to appropriately plead her applications (at [16]), she was unable to identify any powers for some of the orders she sought (at [25] and [27]), and she sought orders which were unintelligible, inactionable or fundamentally flawed (at [34] and [37]). Her lack of success in these applications is self-evident.
The father’s position before the Court is contained in his Amended Application for Final Orders filed 26 May 2025. Thus, the mother has been on notice of his intention to see an order under s102QAC since this time. The mother neglected to address this order in her Amended Response, trial affidavit or during cross-examination. However, the mother had every opportunity to do so. Further, the mother did not address this order in her written submissions. The Court is satisfied that the mother had the opportunity to be heard on this issue in accordance with s 102QAC(5).

Harm experienced by the father

During the course of the father’s litigation history with the mother, he has suffered financial harm and his capacity to care for the children has been affected. The father has been forced to meet various applications which were filed, dealt with by the Court and then refiled by the mother. During the course of the litigation, at times, the mother did not comply with orders of the Court for filing documents, even when such hearings arose out of applications she herself filed. Additionally, during the trial, the mother would abruptly leave the Courtroom and refuse to return causing excessive delay to the proceedings. Further, the mother consistently sent inappropriate correspondence to Chambers despite being informed numerous times of the guidelines for the same.
The Court has made findings earlier in these reasons that the mother displayed behaviour which was paranoid and dysregulated, and these findings are relevant when viewing the conduct of the mother’s litigation.

Conduct of the mother’s litigation

The mother’s applications brought before the Court, attempted to raise the same issue multiple times despite having previously been dealt with by the Court. For example, the mother sought in her Application in a Proceeding filed 5 May 2025, that her supervised time with the children be immediately reinstated. The reason for the refusal of this order is contained at [32]–[38] of Mavridis. The Court declined to reinstate the mother’s time pending the final hearing, as well as “the mother’s lack of insight and protective capacity for her own children” (Mavridis at [35]). Yet, in her Application in a Proceeding filed 4 August 2025, a mere four days prior to the commencement of the final hearing, the mother again sought for her time with the children to be immediately reinstated. The reason for declining this order was set out in Mavridis (No 3) at [24], whereby the Court explains “there was no new evidence to warrant revisiting the same”.
Another example of the mother raising the same issue across more than one application before the Court is where the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding on 4 August 2025 seeking that I recuse myself. I dealt with this application on the first day of the final hearing. All parties were provided with the opportunity to make oral submissions in relation to the mother’s application. I declined to recuse myself as there was no evidence before the Court to justify the same. I further advised the mother that she could appeal this decision if she wished, and that I intended to provide more fulsome reasons at a later date. The next day, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding dated 8 August 2025, which, sought that the “final hearing in this matter be immediately stayed pending determination of the recusal and stay applications filed on 4 August 2025”. The Court directed the mother to paragraph 12 of her accompanying affidavit filed 8 August 2025, where the mother said:
I attended court expecting my recusal and stay applications to be heard. However, his Honour did not hear or rule on those applications. There was no judgment, no formal refusal, no record issued explaining why the applications were not dealt with.

(Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 99 lines 1 –3).
The Court clarified with the mother what exactly she was contending and asked the mother if she did or did not remember the Court dealing with her application for recusal and stay the previous day. The mother responded “I guess so. No problem” and that she “made a mistake” (Transcript 8 August 2025, p. 99 lines 24–28). The Court does not accept that the mother has made a genuine mistake but rather finds that this is merely an example of the way the mother has chosen to conduct her litigation. The mother has consistently made statements which are inconsistent with reality and or objective records and has expressed herself through continuous filings and email correspondence, to both Chambers, and the father.
Each time the mother chose to pursue litigation of this type, it detracted from the central issue within this case, being the children. Additionally, the father incurred further cost by having his legal representatives attend and respond to the mother’s applications and communications. The Court has no doubt that the impact on father’s parenting capacity to care for the children whilst simultaneously responding to the mother’s litigation would have been detrimental.

The cumulative effect of harm to the children of the mother’s conduct

The mother’s conduct during the proceedings caused the first Independent Children’s Lawyer to withdraw from the proceedings. The mother made a range of accusations towards the Independent Children’s Lawyer, including but not limited to, the Independent Children’s Lawyer’s own life circumstances, which were not at all relevant to the present case. The effect of the Independent Children’s Lawyer withdrawing from the proceedings was that it caused the children to be reinterviewed by yet another professional whom they did not have a pre-existing relationship with, and who had not represented their voice throughout the majority of the proceedings. The conduct of the mother in relation to this issue was reprehensible.
The Single Joint Expert remarked that:
... in order to protect and promote the progress that the children have made, to safeguard their critical relationship with their father, to ensure that they do not experience further harm related to the behaviours of their mother, and to reduce the likelihood of recurrent and damaging court involvement for this family, it is recommended that the children continue to live with their father and do not have any further contact of any form with their mother.

(Updated Family Report, paragraph 47).
Much of the harm caused to the children has been discussed earlier in these reasons for judgment and will not be repeated here. The Court is satisfied that the children would experience further psychological harm, in circumstances where the mother is able to continue filing applications in relation to them.

Notification to the father of any application by the mother pursuant to s 102QAE

The Court is required to make a determination pursuant to s 102QAC(7) as to whether the father ought to be notified if the mother makes an application under s 102QAE for leave to institute proceedings and or if the father should merely be advised that an application has been made and the Court has dismissed the same.
The father’s evidence was silent as to his wishes in relation to s 102QAC(7). Therefore, this Court is discharged from any onus to have regard to his wishes pursuant to s 102QAC(8) of the Act. In such circumstances, the Court has ordered that the father only be notified where an application has been made by the mother and dismissed by the Court. The Court has declined notify the father if only an application has been made, in an attempt to spare him any unnecessary stress of possible proceedings, and to enable him to continue to focus on the children. Should the mother be successful in an application pursuant to s 102QAE of the Act for leave to commence proceedings, he will be notified via the service of Court documents in the usual fashion.

LEAVE TO COMMENCE PROCEEDINGS FOR ALTERATION OF PROPERTY INTERESTS OUT OF TIME

On 12 February 2025, the mother filed an Amended Response to Initiating Application which sought an order pursuant to s 79 of the Act.
On 19 February 2025, Campton J noted that “to progress relief adjusting property pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) the mother requires an indulgence by way of leave pursuant to s 44(3) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth)” (Orders made 19 February 2025, Notation G). His Honour went on to order that the mother must file and serve any Amending Response to Initiating Application by 5 March 2025. On 24 February 2025, the mother filed an Amended Response to Final Orders and did not seek any orders relating to the adjustment of property.
However, the mother led evidence relating to property proceedings in her following affidavits:
(a) Affidavit filed 5 May 2025;

(b) Affidavit filed 7 May 2025;

(c) Affidavit filed 20 June 2025; and

(d) Affidavit filed 4 August 2025.

On 7 May 2025, the mother filed an Application in a Proceeding which sought that the former matrimonial home be sold and the proceeds be held in trust. Additionally, the mother sought that the net proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home be split 70/30 in her favour. During the interim hearing, the mother was reminded, once again, that she required an indulgence of the Court to commence property proceedings out of time. I made orders on 20 June 2025, permitting the mother to amend her Application in a Proceeding to include orders which seek leave to commence property proceedings out of time by no later than 11 July 2025, and that if such application was filed, that it would be listed for determination on 7 August 2025, being the commencement of the trial. An order of the Court “should be considered in its entirety on its plain meaning without regard to the subjective intentions of the parties” (Farina & Lofts and Ors [2019] FamCA 228 at [23]). The framing of Order 8 of the orders made 20 June 2025 made it a “guillotine” order. Guillotine orders “have the effect that, once the condition on which the order depends is satisfied, then the sanction for non-compliance with the order becomes effective without the need for further order” (Ronca Constructions Pty Ltd v Ventura [2026] NSWSC 67, citing Marcan Shipping (London) Ltd v Kefalas [2007] EWCA Civ 463 at [11] and [14]). The mother did not file an amended application in accordance with Orders 7 of those made 20 June 2025, and therefore such application filed 7 August 2025 did not form part of the issues at trial.
On 7 August 2025, the mother filed an Amended Response to Final Orders which again sought that the Court make orders relating to the former matrimonial home, namely, an order for the mother to retain exclusive occupation for the same, and to engage in a settlement process.
On 21 November 2025, the mother filed her written submissions, which was the first time the mother properly particularised that she sought leave pursuant to s 44(3) of the Act. The Court does not accept the mere reference to such leave in written submissions as forming part of the issues before the Court. Additionally, should the Court permit the mother to rely on such an order, it would be procedural unfair to the father.
Due to the mother’s failures to comply with the filing directions to particularise her applications so far as they relate to property before the Court, the Court is not satisfied that an application for the same is formally on foot. Indeed, when the Court raised with the father’s legal representatives during closing submissions whether they had any comment in relation to the property orders sought by the mother, they noted “there’s no evidence in relation to anything to do with property” (Transcript 19 November 2025, p. 284 lines 28–29).
The evidence presented by the father did not address any of the mother’s evidence in her affidavits in relation to potential property proceedings, as there were no orders formally on foot which sought leave to commence property proceedings out of time.
In short, the mother contended that the cause of her delay in bringing an application for property adjustment under s 79 of the Act was her inadequate legal representation. She contended that the hardship caused to her was being excluded from the opportunity to seek a just and equitable order altering the property interests of the father and herself. In circumstances where she claims that she made a not insubstantial initial financial contribution, her case seems prima facie to be an arguable one, however this is not an application which is formally before the Court at this time. It is for this reason that the Court intends to make a carve out to the harmful proceedings order above made which will allow the mother to file an application to formally seek leave to commence property proceedings out of time. Whether or not such leave will be granted will be at the discretion of the judicial officer who manages the application.
The Court cannot discern why such an application should come before Division 1 of this Court. It should be filed in the normal fashion, in Division 2.
In passing, the mother should understand that irrespective of her claim for contribution, the father also made contributions and, significantly in this case, has continued to make contributions for many years after separation. Moreover, it is possible that the considerations relating to current and future circumstances set out in s 79(5) of the Act significantly favour the father in circumstances where he is solely responsible for the care of four children with special needs. Thus, the mother is encouraged to lower her expectations about her entitlement lest she find that any potential costs awarded against her exceed the value of that entitlement.

CONCLUSION

The Court has done the best it can to formulate orders which will minimise any dysregulation to the children and protect their therapeutic progress.
